Human Factors.q: What role does professionalism and ethics play in human factors?
Human Factors.a: Professionalism and ethics contribute in the reduction of introduced errors.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.q: Describe the steps for a dye-penetrant inspection.
Inspection Concepts and Techniques.a: The surface is cleaned, the penetrant is applied, and allowed to dwell for a specified time.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.q: What are some of the non-destructive testing methods that may be used on aluminum components?
Inspection Concepts and Techniques.a: Visual, dye-penetrant, eddy-current, ultrasonic testing, and radiography.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.q: What method would you use to inspect wood structures for rot?
Inspection Concepts and Techniques.a: Use a visual inspection using a light to inspect wood structures for rot.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.q: Although a dent on the outside of an aircraft may be evident, what other damage may not be obvious?
Inspection Concepts and Techniques.a: Distortion or failure of the substructure may be hidden and not be apparent from the outside of the aircraft.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.q: What may result if Appendix D of Part 43 is not referenced when developing your own checklist to perform a 100-hour inspection?
Inspection Concepts and Techniques.a: The inspection may miss required items or contain unnecessary items.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.q: Which non-destructive testing method can be performed without removing the surface coatings such as primer, paint, anodized films, and is effective in detecting surface and subsurface corrosion?
Inspection Concepts and Techniques.a: Eddy-current inspection method.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.q: It is important that an inspection for corrosion includes what sides of an aircraft structure?
Inspection Concepts and Techniques.a: Both the outside and inside of the aircraft structure.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.q: What occurs during the penetration stage when the penetrant liquid is applied to and then removed from a part that is cracked?
Inspection Concepts and Techniques.a: The penetrant enters the crack and remains there.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.q: When the developer is applied, what happens?
Inspection Concepts and Techniques.a: The crack becomes more visible.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.q: On a wood structure, why is it necessary to examine all stains and discoloration carefully?
Inspection Concepts and Techniques.a: To determine whether they are harmless stains or signs of preliminary or advanced decay.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.q: A correctly made butt weld will have what bead width and how much penetration?
Inspection Concepts and Techniques.a: The bead width should be three to five times the thickness of the base metal, with 100% penetration.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.q: What are the characteristics of a cold weld?
Inspection Concepts and Techniques.a: Rough or irregular edges not feathered into the base metal, and variations in penetration amounts.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.q: The penetration of a fillet weld should be what percentage of the thickness of the base metal?
Describe the basic steps for conducting a magnetic particle inspection.
Inspection Concepts and Techniques.a: Magnetize the part, then coat the surface with ferromagnetic particles. Defects cause a visible particle pattern.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.q: What types of operations require that aircraft operating under 14 CFR Part 91 must undergo 100-hour inspections?
Inspection Concepts and Techniques.a: Aircraft operated for compensation or hire, or used for flight instruction when the aircraft is provided by the instructor.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.q: What can be missed if only a visual inspection is performed on major structural members?
Inspection Concepts and Techniques.a: Suspected internal cracks or damage.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.q: An aircraft is flown four hours over the 100-hour inspection limit to reach a maintenance facility. How will you account for those hours?
Inspection Concepts and Techniques.a: They are included in the next 100-hour time-in-service period.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.q: If required, what inspection must be performed every 12 calendar months?
Inspection Concepts and Techniques.a: An annual inspection.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.q: An aircraft on a progressive inspection program is operated for compensation or hire. Does it need a 100-hour inspection?
Inspection Concepts and Techniques.a: No, progressive inspections replace 100-hour inspections.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.q: When can an aircraft with an expired annual inspection be flown to a base for the inspection?
Inspection Concepts and Techniques.a: When a special flight permit has been issued by the FAA.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.q: When a progressive inspection program is discontinued, when is the next 100-hour inspection due?
Inspection Concepts and Techniques.a: 100 hours after the last complete inspection.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.q: An owner desires to use a progressive inspection program. To whom do they apply?
Inspection Concepts and Techniques.a: The FAA Flight Standards District Office (FSDO) with jurisdiction over the area.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.q: Is an engine run-up required as part of a 100-hour inspection on a turbine-powered aircraft?
Inspection Concepts and Techniques.a: Yes.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.q: Where would you locate the required items that must be recorded in the aircraft maintenance records following completion of a 100-hour inspection?
Inspection Concepts and Techniques.a: 14 CFR Part 43.11.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.q: Where might you locate a sample of the recommended statement for recording the approval for return to service of an aircraft after an annual or 100-hour inspection?
Inspection Concepts and Techniques.a: 14 CFR Part 43.11.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.q: Eddy current and ultrasonic testing are used to find what type of damage to an aircraft structure?
Inspection Concepts and Techniques.a: Hidden damage.

Mathematics.q: Describe the result of adding a large positive number and a smaller negative number.
Mathematics.a: The result is a smaller positive number.

Mathematics.q: What are positive and negative numbers called?
Mathematics.a: They are called either signed numbers or integers.

Mathematics.q: What is a mixed number?
Mathematics.a: A combination of a whole number and a fraction.

Mathematics.q: What are the two types of measurement systems used in aviation maintenance?
Mathematics.a: The conventional U.S. (English) system and the metric system.

Mathematics.q: How do you convert a fraction into a decimal?
Mathematics.a: Divide the numerator by the denominator.

Mathematics.q: What is the key step that must be performed in order to add or subtract unlike fractions?
Mathematics.a: Find the lowest common denominator.

Mathematics.q: What is the order of operation for algebraic equations?
Mathematics.a: Parenthesis, exponents, multiplication, division, addition, subtraction.

Mathematics.q: How do you divide one fraction by another?
Mathematics.a: Invert the divisor and multiply the numerators together and the denominators together.

Mathematics.q: How do you convert a decimal into a percent?
Mathematics.a: Multiply the decimal by 100 and add the percent sign, or move the decimal two places to the right and add the percent sign.

Mathematics.q: Name the four basic mathematic functions for whole numbers.
Mathematics.a: Addition, subtraction, multiplication, division.

Mathematics.q: How do you convert a fraction into a percent?
Mathematics.a: Convert it to a decimal, move the decimal point two places to the right, and add the percent sign.

Mathematics.q: How do you change a decimal number into the nearest equivalent fraction?
Mathematics.a: Multiply the decimal by the desired denominator; the result becomes the numerator.

Mathematics.q: What is the formula used to determine the area of a tapered wing?
Mathematics.a: Wing area = wing span × mean average wing chord.

Mathematics.q: What is a ratio?
Mathematics.a: A comparison of two numbers or quantities.

Mathematics.q: What are two different ways that a ratio may be expressed?
Mathematics.a: As a fraction, with a colon, or by using the word “to.”

Mathematics.q: The volume of a rectangular shape is identified in what unit of measure?
Mathematics.a: Cubic units.

Mathematics.q: What is a proportion?
Mathematics.a: A statement of equality between two or more ratios.

Mathematics.q: Define the term chord as it refers to an airplane wing.
Mathematics.a: The average width of the wing from leading edge to trailing edge.

Mathematics.q: What is meant by the root of a number?
Mathematics.a: Two or more equal smaller numbers that, when multiplied together, equal the specified number.

Mathematics.q: How do you find the square of a number? Give an example.
Mathematics.a: Multiply the number by itself. Example: 4 × 4 = 16 or 7 × 7 = 49.

Mathematics.q: What is a common method of making computations involving very large or very small numbers more manageable?
Mathematics.a: Convert numbers into scientific notation using powers of 10.

Mathematics.q: How is 100,000 expressed as a power of 10?
On a drawing, the decimal number 0.33 has a bar over the number. What does the bar mean?
Mathematics.a: The number beneath the bar is repeating to infinity.

Mathematics.q: What is the formula used to find the area of a rectangle?
Mathematics.a: Area = length × width.

Mathematics.q: What formula is used to find the area of a triangle?
Mathematics.a: Area = ½ × base × height.

Mathematics.q: What is the formula used in calculating the piston displacement of a cylinder?
Mathematics.a: Piston displacement = (π × diameter² ÷ 4) × stroke × number of cylinders.

Mathematics.q: What is the formula to find the area of a circle?
Mathematics.a: Area = π × radius².

Mathematics.q: Name the six different types of triangles.
Mathematics.a: Equilateral, isosceles, acute, right, obtuse, scalene.

Mathematics.q: What is a parallelogram?
Mathematics.a: A four-sided figure with two pairs of parallel sides.

Mathematics.q: What is an equilateral triangle?
Mathematics.a: A triangle with three equal sides.

Mathematics.q: What is the hypotenuse in a right triangle?
Mathematics.a: The side opposite the right angle.

Mathematics.q: Describe a trapezoid.
Mathematics.a: A closed four-sided figure having two parallel sides and two non-parallel sides.

Mathematics.q: How do you find the volume of a cylinder?
Mathematics.a: Volume = π × radius² × height.

Physics.q: Define matter.
Physics.a: Any substance that occupies space and has mass.

Physics.q: Matter may exist in what three states?
What is meant by pressure?
Physics.a: Force that acts on a unit of area.

Physics.q: Define kinetic energy.
Physics.a: Kinetic energy is energy in motion.

Physics.q: Work is the product of what two factors?
What are the parts of a lever?
Physics.a: A rigid bar and a pivot point called a fulcrum.

Physics.q: A wheelbarrow is an example of what class of lever?
What is meant by the fulcrum of a lever?
Physics.a: The point about which the lever rotates.

Physics.q: A seesaw is an example of what class of lever?
What is meant by the density of the air?
Physics.a: The weight of a given volume of air.

Physics.q: What characteristic of the atmosphere determines the speed of sound?
Are liquids compressible?
Are all three states of matter affected by thermal expansion, and if so, which state is affected most?
Physics.a: All three states are affected, with gases being affected the most.

Physics.q: What is a British thermal unit (BTU)?
Physics.a: The amount of heat energy required to change the temperature of one pound of water by one degree Fahrenheit.

Physics.q: Name the three methods of heat transfer.
What term defines the temperature at which all molecular motion ceases?
What is meant by the term absolute zero?
Physics.a: The temperature at which all molecular motion ceases.

Physics.q: What are the customary units used to express hydraulic or pneumatic pressure in the U.S.?
Describe how pressure is transmitted when a force is applied to a confined fluid.
Physics.a: Pressure is transmitted equally in all directions.

Physics.q: What formula expresses the relationship between force, pressure, and area?
What is the approximate speed of sound at sea level on a standard day?
What is the atmospheric pressure at sea level on a standard day?
Physics.a: 29.92 inches of mercury, 14.7 PSI, or 1013.2 millibars.

Physics.q: What is the temperature on a standard day at sea level?
What are the factors that determine density altitude?
What is the term used to describe the ratio of actual atmospheric moisture to the amount the air could hold if saturated?
What are the four principal forces acting on an airplane in flight?
What is the term that describes the angle between the relative wind and the chord line of wean airfoil?
What causes an airplane wing to stall?
Physics.a: Stall occurs when the angle of attack exceeds a critical value and airflow separates from the upper wing surface, becoming turbulent.

Physics.q: What are the principal effects of lowering the flaps in flight?
Physics.a: Lift increases, drag increases, and stall speed decreases.

Physics.q: What is the function of a wing-mounted vortex generator?
Physics.a: Vortex generators delay or prevent airflow separation from the upper wing surface during high-speed flight.

Physics.q: How does blade flapping compensate for dissymmetry of lift in helicopter main rotor systems?
Physics.a: Blade flapping increases angle of attack and lift on the retreating blade, and decreases them on the advancing blade.

Physics.q: How does airflow through a helicopter main rotor change during transition from normal flight to autorotation?
Physics.a: In normal flight, air moves downward; in autorotation, air moves upward.

Physics.q: What is friction?
Physics.a: The opposition to relative motion between two objects in contact.

Physics.q: Name the stresses that act on an aircraft.
Physics.a: Tension, compression, torsion, bending, and shear.

Physics.q: What term describes the rate of speed in a direction?
Name the temperature scales used in physics.
Name the forms of energy that can be converted into heat energy.
Physics.a: Mechanical, electrical, chemical, radiant, nuclear, and solar energy.

Physics.q: What happens to pressure and velocity when air/fluid passes through the converging section of a venturi?
What is the standard used to compare the densities of solids and liquids?
What type of stability does wing dihedral provide?
What is the ratio of an aircraft’s speed to the speed of sound called?
What wing design feature adds an upward lateral angle aiding in lateral stability?
What are the three axes of an aircraft?
Is the reaction of airflow over a wing the same as pressure changes in a duct?
Physics.a: Airflow over a wing is represented by pressure changes in a converging duct.

Physics.q: What control surface moves the aircraft about its vertical axis?
What control surface moves the aircraft about its lateral axis?
What control surface moves the aircraft about its longitudinal axis?
A single fixed pulley represents which class of lever?
In a first-class lever, where is the fulcrum located?
What is the formula for power?
How is power converted to horsepower?
Physics.a: Divide power by the appropriate conversion factor based on units.

Physics.q: What is Bernoulli’s principle of subsonic airflow?
Physics.a: As fluid velocity increases, static pressure decreases, provided no energy is added or removed.

Physics.q: What is the acute angle between the wing chord and the aircraft’s longitudinal axis called?

Weight and Balance.q: If an aircraft is loaded so that the aft CG limit is exceeded, what undesirable flight characteristic is likely to result?
Weight and Balance.a: The aircraft will be tail-heavy and may be unable to recover from a stall or spin.

Weight and Balance.q: If all the records for an aircraft are missing, how would you determine the empty weight and empty weight CG?
Weight and Balance.a: The aircraft would have to be weighed, and new weight and balance records would have to be prepared.

Weight and Balance.q: Define “arm” as used in aircraft weight and balance calculations.
Weight and Balance.a: The horizontal distance from the datum to any point within the aircraft.

Weight and Balance.q: Define tare weight and describe how it is handled when weighing an aircraft.
Weight and Balance.a: Tare weight is the weight of anything on the scales that is not part of the aircraft. It must be subtracted from the scale reading to determine the net weight.

Weight and Balance.q: If a piece of equipment such as a radio is added to an aircraft, how can you determine the effect on weight and balance without reweighing the aircraft?
Weight and Balance.a: If records are up to date, a new weight and CG can be determined by computation.

Weight and Balance.q: Why is control of the weight and balance of an aircraft important?
Weight and Balance.a: For the safety and efficiency of flight.

Weight and Balance.q: What is the datum of an aircraft and what is its function?
Weight and Balance.a: An imaginary vertical plane at right angles to the longitudinal axis of the airplane; the reference point for all horizontal measurements.

Weight and Balance.q: How do you determine the moment of an item of equipment?
Weight and Balance.a: Multiply its weight by its arm (distance from datum).

Weight and Balance.q: Why is accurate, up-to-date empty weight CG data important?
Weight and Balance.a: All other weight and balance calculations are based on the empty weight CG.

Weight and Balance.q: What are two ways of determining the arm of an item of equipment?
Weight and Balance.a: Measure the distance between the item and the datum, or use manufacturer data.

Weight and Balance.q: What is a plumb bob used for?
Weight and Balance.a: To determine a spot perpendicular to the point from which it hangs.

Weight and Balance.q: What should be done to obtain a positive moment after the datum?
Weight and Balance.a: Add weight.

Weight and Balance.q: What should be done to obtain a negative moment after the datum?
Weight and Balance.a: Remove weight.

Weight and Balance.q: What should be done to obtain a positive moment forward of the datum?
Weight and Balance.a: Remove weight.

Weight and Balance.q: What does the CG location indicate?
Weight and Balance.a: The point at which the aircraft is balanced.

Weight and Balance.q: What information is required in a weight and balance report?
Weight and Balance.a: Empty weight and empty weight CG.

Weight and Balance.q: What should be done to obtain a negative moment forward of the datum?
Weight and Balance.a: Add weight.

Weight and Balance.q: If the empty weight CG is listed as “none” on the aircraft specification/type certificate, what must be done after an equipment change?
Weight and Balance.a: A check must be made to ensure that no legal loading combination can move the CG outside limits.

Weight and Balance.q: Why is it important to recalculate weight and balance after an equipment change?
Weight and Balance.a: New empty weight and CG must be determined to ensure limits are not exceeded.

Weight and Balance.q: Who is responsible for revising the equipment list after an equipment change?
Weight and Balance.a: The mechanic or repairman who makes the change.

Weight and Balance.q: How does the category of an aircraft affect its loaded weight?
Weight and Balance.a: Aircraft certificated in more than one category may have different maximum gross weights and useful loads.

Weight and Balance.q: What should be done with equipment not normally carried in flight when weighing an aircraft?
Weight and Balance.a: Remove all items not regularly carried in flight.

Weight and Balance.q: What should you do with equipment not permanently installed or recorded when weighing an aircraft?
Weight and Balance.a: Remove it before weighing.

Weight and Balance.q: What is the aerodynamic effect of CG forward or aft of the limits?
Weight and Balance.a: Out-of-balance condition, nose-down or nose-up tendency.

Weight and Balance.q: Define residual fuel.
Weight and Balance.a: Fuel remaining after the system has been drained (in tanks, lines, engines).

Weight and Balance.q: At what attitude are aircraft placed to be weighed?
Weight and Balance.a: According to manufacturer’s procedures; generally in level flight attitude.

Weight and Balance.q: What weight and balance info is in aircraft specifications but not type certificate data sheets?
Weight and Balance.a: An equipment list.

Weight and Balance.q: What does the manufacturer’s equipment list specify?
Weight and Balance.a: All required and approved equipment.

Weight and Balance.q: Define residual oil.
Weight and Balance.a: Oil remaining after the system has been drained.

Weight and Balance.q: Why is a hydrometer used regarding weight and balance?
Weight and Balance.a: To determine the specific gravity (weight per gallon) of fuel.

Weight and Balance.q: What should be done with fuel when weighing an aircraft?
Weight and Balance.a: Drain until only unusable fuel remains, or fill full and subtract usable fuel.

Weight and Balance.q: What weight and balance items are listed in the type certificate data sheet?
Weight and Balance.a: CG ranges, max weight, leveling means, seat arms, baggage capacity/arm, fuel capacity/arm, datum, engine horsepower, oil capacity/arm, residual fuel/oil amounts.

Weight and Balance.q: Where are leveling instructions found, and name one leveling method.
Weight and Balance.a: Found in type certificate data sheets; use spirit level at points or plumb bob with scale/protractor.

Weight and Balance.q: Define mean aerodynamic chord (MAC).
Weight and Balance.a: The chord through the center of the wing plane area.

Weight and Balance.q: Describe two ways of showing CG location/range.
Weight and Balance.a: In inches from datum or in % of MAC.

Weight and Balance.q: What is the purpose of an aircraft loading graph?
Weight and Balance.a: To determine load distribution to keep CG within limits.

Weight and Balance.q: What is ballast and why is it used?
Weight and Balance.a: Weight added to bring CG into allowable range.

Weight and Balance.q: How should ballast be attached?
Weight and Balance.a: Rigidly, without interfering with controls, so it can’t be dislodged.

Weight and Balance.q: How should permanent ballast be marked?
Weight and Balance.a: “Permanent Ballast – Do Not Remove” in red letters.

Weight and Balance.q: How should temporary ballast be marked?
Weight and Balance.a: Marked with its weight and “Removal Requires W&B Check.”

Weight and Balance.q: If new radios in the nose cause forward CG limit to be exceeded, how can it be corrected without removing them?
Weight and Balance.a: Install ballast in the tail.

Weight and Balance.q: Explain a forward adverse loaded CG check.
Weight and Balance.a: Load all items in front of forward CG limit, leave aft items empty, ensure CG falls within limits.

Weight and Balance.q: Explain a rearward adverse loaded CG check.
Weight and Balance.a: Load items aft of CG limit, leave forward items empty, ensure CG falls within limits.

Weight and Balance.q: How does CG range of a helicopter compare to a fixed-wing?
Weight and Balance.a: Helicopters have a much smaller CG range.

Weight and Balance.q: Which has the more critical CG range, airplane or helicopter?
Weight and Balance.a: Helicopter.

Weight and Balance.q: Before a helicopter is weighed, in what directions must it be leveled?
Weight and Balance.a: Longitudinally and laterally.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: What causes an AC motor to run too fast?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Excessive applied voltage or shorted field windings.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: How are DC generators connected to allow them both to power all electrical loads?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: The generators operate in parallel.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: What tool or tools are used to check a motor or generator armature for shorts under load conditions?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: A growler.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: When using a growler, what indication will occur when a short exists between an armature segment?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: A metal blade held over the armature will vibrate and chatter.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: On small turbine engines, what device is used for both starting the engine and producing electrical power?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Starter-generator.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: How is the output voltage of an alternator controlled?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: The generator control unit varies the field current.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: What does the voltage regulator control to regulate alternator output voltage?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: The generator/alternator field current.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: What instruments can be used to monitor electrical system loads?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Multimeters, Ammeters, and Voltmeters.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: How can a specific wire be identified in a wire bundle?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: By the identification code marked on the wire.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: Why is it necessary to use flux in silver soldering?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Flux chemically cleans the base metal.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: When supporting wire bundles, what is the maximum spacing interval of clamps?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Not exceeding 24 inches.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: What tool is used to determine the size of an unmarked wire?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Wire gauge.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: What is the go-to method for charging NICAD batteries?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Constant current charging method.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: To join electrical connections, what type of solder is used for good connection and low resistance?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Soft solder.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: What is a constant speed drive control?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Keeps AC alternator at specific RPM and controls frequency.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: When clamping RF cables, what are the determining factors for clamp selection?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Must fit snugly, not crush, and allow slight axial movement.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: What color are the navigation lights on the wingtips?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Left = red, Right = green.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: Name several electrical devices that would be considered intermittent loads.
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Landing gear motors, cowl/wing flap motors, radio transmitters.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: What law of electricity is used in troubleshooting electrical systems?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Ohm’s law.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: What types of circuit current require derating of a switch?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: High inrush current and inductive circuits (coils, relays).

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: What varies the current flow in a generator system?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Voltage regulator.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: Why analyze an electrical system before increasing the load of the system?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: To ensure generator, wire, and protective device capacity is not exceeded.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: What factors determine wire size for a specific application?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Mechanical strength, allowable power loss, permissible voltage drop, current carrying ability.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: What is the purpose of an inverter in an electrical system?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Converts DC to AC.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: What mil-spec splice is used in moisture and wind areas?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Environmentally sealed splices conforming to Milspec T-7928.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: Why are switches derated when used with DC motors?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: DC motors draw several times rated current at start.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: How should splices be placed in bundles?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Splices staggered to reduce bundle size.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: What inspection should be performed, after a wire harness is installed?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Check for slack between the last clamp and equipment to prevent strain.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: What is the relationship between conduit diameter and wire bundle diameter?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Conduit must be 25% larger than the bundle.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: Copper vs. aluminum wire – what are the considerations?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Aluminum is lighter and often used when weight is important.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: Name the colors and locations of position lights required by regulation.
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Red (left wingtip), Green (right wingtip), White (tail/vertical stabilizer).

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: What should be done to conduit ends when fittings are not used?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Deburr to prevent wire insulation damage.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: What diagrams are used to troubleshoot complex electrical systems?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Block diagrams and schematics.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: Maximum number of terminal lugs on one terminal strip stud?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Four.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: Name five types of exterior aircraft lighting.
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Position, anti-collision, landing, taxi, and wing inspection lights.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: Most common materials in soft solder alloys?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Tin and lead.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: Why is soft solder used for electrical connections?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Provides strong union with low resistance.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: How can inadequate bonding/grounding cause unreliability?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Allows static discharge/lightning to damage sensitive equipment.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: What three faults are associated with electrical system malfunctions?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Open circuits, shorted circuits, low power.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: Proper length for a bonding jumper?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: As short as practical, but long enough to allow free movement.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: What should be done with a switch used with high inrush/inductive/DC motor circuits?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: The switch should be derated.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: When must a switch be derated from its nominal rating?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Inductive circuits, high inrush circuits, and DC motors.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: At what point do electric motors draw the most current?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: At startup.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: Why is shielded wire used in some systems?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: To eliminate electromagnetic interference.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: Circuit breakers are designed to open before what occurs?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Overload or circuit fault.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: At what point in a circuit should the protective device be located?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: As close to the power source as possible.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: How are composite aircraft structures protected from lightning?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: With a conductive material layer.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: What type of circuit breaker may not be used in an aircraft?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Automatic resetting type.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: What part of the aircraft is identified by the white navigation light?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Tail, visible from the rear.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: How does a strobe light produce light?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: High-voltage capacitor discharge.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: What could cause an AC motor to run too slowly?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Lack of lubrication, defective wiring, or low voltage.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: What should be done when making bonding/grounding connections between dissimilar materials?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Use a suitable washer so corrosion occurs on the washer.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: Before making a continuity check, what must be done to an electrical circuit?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: Power must be turned off.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.q: What is a SWAMP connector, and where are they used sometimes?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.a: A connector used in Severe Wind & Moisture Problem areas. SWAMP connectors are usually near wheel wells, wing flaps, wing folds, pylons, and other exterior areas that may have a harsh environment.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: Why do some aircraft have fuel jettison systems?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: To allow the crew to reduce the weight of the aircraft down to or below the maximum allowable landing weight.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: Is there any reason why a fuel jettison system might be required on a small aircraft?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: Yes, if the maximum takeoff weight exceeds the maximum allowable landing weight, a jettison system would be required.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: What are some other names for a single-point fueling system?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: An underwing or pressure fueling system.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: What is a sight glass as it relates to aircraft fuel systems?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: A sight glass is a clear glass or plastic tube open to the fuel tank that fills with fuel to the same level as the fuel in the tank.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: What would tell the pilot that the fuel filter has gone into bypass?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: The enunciator light is illuminated on the panel due to high differential pressure.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: Why do multi-engine airplanes have fuel cross-feed systems?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: To allow any engine to draw fuel from any tank.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: The fuel selector valve for a multi-engine aircraft must have at least three positions. What are they?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: On, Off, and Crossfeed.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: Why do some fuel tanks have internal baffles?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: To resist fuel surging or sloshing caused by changes in the altitude of the aircraft.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: Name the different types of fuel cells.
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: Removable rigid, bladder, and integral fuel cells.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: What does the term “wet wing” mean?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: It means that sealed portions of the aircraft wing structure are used for the fuel tank (also called an integral tank).

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: What is the primary means for monitoring fuel delivery to the engines?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: The fuel flow indicator.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: How is the weight of the fuel supported when bladder type fuel cells are used?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: The bladder is supported by the aircraft’s structure which contains it.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: Why do turbine engine aircraft have fuel temperature indicating systems?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: To allow the crew to determine if the fuel is cold enough to produce a danger of the formation of ice crystals.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: Defueling a transport category aircraft can be done utilizing the aircraft’s boost pumps or by what other means?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: By using the pump in the refueling truck.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: Define what a baffle check valve does.
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: These valves allow fuel to move to the low inboard sections of the tank but prevent it from moving outboard. They ensure fuel boost pumps always have fuel regardless of the aircraft’s attitude.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: Name four types of fuel quantity gauging systems currently in use.
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: Sight gauges, mechanical, electric, and electronic gauges.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: What are the four basic classifications used to describe aircraft fuel leaks?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: Stain, Seep, Heavy Seep, and Running Leak.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: Why are electronic capacitance-type fuel quantity indicating systems more accurate than other types?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: They measure the mass of the fuel instead of volume.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: What does microbial growth in turbine engine fuels look like?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: A visible slime that is dark brown, green, grey, red, or black in color.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: Regarding fuel quantity indicators in transport category airplanes, what must be provided to the crew?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: Indication of usable fuel in gallons (or equivalent units) in each tank during flight. If unusable fuel exceeds 1 gallon or 5% of tank capacity, a red arc must be marked on the indicator.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: What should be done to a bladder fuel cell when it must be stored without fuel for an extended period?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: Wipe the inside of the bladder with a coating of clean engine oil.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: What are drip gauges and sight gauges?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: Underwing, bayonet-type fuel gauges.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: Micron filters capture particles in what size range?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: 10 to 25 microns.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: What types of aircraft use variable capacitance type fuel quantity indicating systems?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: Large and high-performance turbine aircraft.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: Explain the major differences between a fuel strainer and a fuel filter.
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: Fuel strainers are coarse mesh designed to trap large debris and do not inhibit water flow. Fuel filters use fine mesh and can trap water.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: What is the purpose of an in-transit light associated with an electrically operated fuel tank shutoff valve?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: To indicate that the valve is in motion between positions.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: What are the three types of hand-operated valves used in aircraft fuel systems?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: Cone type, poppet type, and gate valve.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: During an emergency, what is used on some aircraft to lower gross weight to allowable landing weight?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: The fuel jettison or dump system.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: On a fuel gate valve, what relieves excess pressure buildup due to temperature increases?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: A thermal relief bypass valve.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: Why is a fuel jettison system usually divided into two independent systems?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: To maintain lateral stability by jettisoning fuel from a heavy wing if necessary.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: What procedures should be followed regarding gaskets and seals when replacing fuel system components?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: All old gaskets and seals should be replaced.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: Why is microbial growth a potential issue with turbine engine fuel?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: Turbine engine fuel always contains some amount of water.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: Can a fuel system develop a leak without visible evidence such as a stain or spot?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: Yes, internal components such as shutoff valves, crossfeed valves, or selector valves can leak without being visible.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: How is a fuel tank checked for leaks after a patch or welded repair?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: Slightly pressurize the tank with air and use a soap and water solution to check.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: Why should you wait a period of time after fueling before checking fuel sumps?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: To allow water and sediment to settle to the drain point.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: What is the worst form of contamination that may be found in fuel?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: Fuel contaminated with another type of fuel not intended for use in that aircraft.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: What is the most common form of contamination found in fuel?
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: Water.

Aircraft Fuel Systems.q: Name some advantages of a single-point fueling system.
Aircraft Fuel Systems.a: Reduces fueling time, reduces chances of contamination, reduces possible fire from static electricity, and eliminates damage to the aircraft’s skin.

Aircraft Instrument Systems.q: A static check may be performed on a manifold pressure gauge by using what piece of information?
What aircraft instrument can be used to check a manifold pressure gauge for proper indication?
Name several of the indication errors that may be found in altimeters.
Aircraft Instrument Systems.a: Scale errors, hysteresis errors, friction errors, and installation errors.

Aircraft Instrument Systems.q: What is the signal source in an electrical resistance-type temperature indicating system?
What type of temperature sensing system is normally used to measure a turbine engine exhaust gas temperature?
What operating parameter is indicated by a tachometer?
Aircraft Instrument Systems.a: Engine RPM, crankshaft, or turbine rotor speed.

Aircraft Instrument Systems.q: What is the name of the effect that causes a gyro to respond to an applied force at a point 90° further in the direction of rotation?
What is the maximum deviation allowed for a magnetic compass?
What action should be taken if the fluid level is found low during the inspection of a float-type whiskey compass?
Aircraft Instrument Systems.a: The compass must be replaced or serviced by an instrument repair station.

Aircraft Instrument Systems.q: Where would you find the calibration requirements for compasses?
What are three sources of power used to drive a gyroscopic turn indicator?
Aircraft Instrument Systems.a: Air from a vacuum pump, a pressure source, or an electric motor.

Aircraft Instrument Systems.q: What items should be considered during the inspection of a magnetic compass?
Aircraft Instrument Systems.a: The fluid must be clear, housing full with no bubbles, card legible, lubber line intact, and compass calibrated.

Aircraft Instrument Systems.q: In what way do magnetic fields generated by electrical equipment and steel parts in the airplane affect the magnetic compass?
Aircraft Instrument Systems.a: They cause stray magnetic fields that deflect the compass from correct alignment with the earth’s magnetic field.

Aircraft Instrument Systems.q: Describe the basic operating concept of a synchro-type remote indicating system.
Aircraft Instrument Systems.a: A remote transmitter electrically signals a receiver inside the instrument.

Aircraft Instrument Systems.q: What aircraft instruments are connected to the pitot-static system?
Aircraft Instrument Systems.a: Altimeter, vertical speed indicator, airspeed indicator, and Mach meter (in high-performance aircraft).

Aircraft Instrument Systems.q: Why should you avoid using a tool to clean obstructions from the sensing holes in a pitot-static system?
After components in a pitot-static system have been replaced, what tests or inspections are required?
Aircraft Instrument Systems.a: The static system leak test (per FAR 91.411).

Aircraft Instrument Systems.q: What quantity is accurately measured by a capacitance-type fuel quantity indicating system?
Aircraft Instrument Systems.a: The mass or weight of the fuel.

Aircraft Instrument Systems.q: Who is authorized to apply the range markings to an instrument dial face?
Aircraft Instrument Systems.a: The manufacturer or a certificated instrument repair station.

Aircraft Instrument Systems.q: Why are many aircraft instrument panels installed with shock mounts?
Aircraft Instrument Systems.a: They absorb low-frequency, high-amplitude shocks, and vertical/horizontal vibrations.

Aircraft Instrument Systems.q: What is the purpose of a slippage mark on an instrument glass?
Aircraft Instrument Systems.a: To indicate if the glass has moved, which could cause incorrect range markings.

Aircraft Instrument Systems.q: What are the two common methods of mounting instruments?
Aircraft Instrument Systems.a: Flange/bezel mount and flangeless clamp mount.

Aircraft Instrument Systems.q: What references should be consulted to determine the proper range markings for aircraft instruments?
Aircraft Instrument Systems.a: Type certificate data sheets, aircraft specifications, flight manual, or maintenance manual.

Aircraft Instrument Systems.q: What is the meaning of the yellow arc on an aircraft instrument?
Where may the alternate static source be on unpressurized aircraft?
Why are fuselage-mounted static ports placed on both sides of the aircraft?
Aircraft Instrument Systems.a: To compensate for variations in static air pressure caused by aircraft attitude.

Aircraft Instrument Systems.q: What is the reason for swinging a compass, and how is it accomplished?
Aircraft Instrument Systems.a: To compensate for deviations from magnetic fields in the aircraft, accomplished by adjusting compensating magnets.

Aircraft Instrument Systems.q: What is the purpose of the master caution warning light?
Aircraft Instrument Systems.a: To alert the crew to a critical situation requiring immediate attention and direct them to another enunciator.

Aircraft Instrument Systems.q: How is a sensitive altimeter set to measure pressure altitude?
Aircraft Instrument Systems.a: By setting the barometric pressure to 29.92.

Aircraft Instrument Systems.q: In what units are reciprocating engine manifold pressure indicators calibrated?
What is the name of the sensing device used to measure high temperatures, such as reciprocating cylinder head temperatures?
A two-component electrically operated system capable of transmitting information such as position or pressure to an indicator in the cockpit is called what?
What is the characteristic of a gyro where the rotor remains spinning in its original plane regardless of base position?
What pressure system is the altimeter connected to?
How does bonding help reduce radio interference?
Aircraft Instrument Systems.a: By minimizing static charges and providing a low-impedance ground return.

Aircraft Instrument Systems.q: By what methods may the gyro of a turn-and-bank/slip indicator be driven?
What are the two types of pneumatically driven gyro systems?
When installing a pressure-sensing instrument, what precaution must be taken?
Aircraft Instrument Systems.a: Ensure fittings are properly installed to reduce fluid leaks.

Aircraft Instrument Systems.q: What type of vacuum pump system requires the use of an air-oil separator?
Aircraft Instrument Systems.a: An engine-driven vacuum pump lubricated by engine oil.

Aircraft Instrument Systems.q: How much vacuum is usually required for proper instrument operation?
What should be done to all metal parts of the aircraft to prevent the development of electrical potential buildup?
A capacitance-type fuel quantity system is calibrated in what units?
What is the function of cathode ray tubes in an electronic flight instrument system?
Aircraft Instrument Systems.a: To display alphanumeric data and visual instrument representations.

Aircraft Instrument Systems.q: Shielding, isolation, bonding, and static discharge wicks are all methods to minimize what interference?
What does the acronym BIT (B-I-T) mean when referring to avionics equipment?
What does the acronym EFIS stand for?
What is a display system that projects primary flight information on a transparent screen in the pilot’s forward field of view called?
On an IFR-certificated aircraft under 14 CFR Part 91, how often must a pitot-static system leak check be conducted?
Why would a dent in the fuselage near a static port cause an altimeter error?
Aircraft Instrument Systems.a: It disrupts airflow, causing incorrect static pressure.

Aircraft Instrument Systems.q: What stall warning device is activated by low-pressure airflow over a wing leading edge opening?
Sensing angle of attack (AOA) is important for what aircraft warning system?
What is the purpose of the aircraft aural (oral) warning system?
Aircraft Instrument Systems.a: To audibly inform the pilot of a situation requiring attention.

Aircraft Instrument Systems.q: How is a sensitive altimeter set to measure barometric pressure?
Aircraft Instrument Systems.a: By setting the altimeter to field elevation and reading barometric pressure in the window.

Airframe Inspection.q: Where do you find the regulation that specifies the operation conditions that make 100-hour inspections mandatory?
Airframe Inspection.a: 14 CFR Part 91-409

Airframe Inspection.q: A certificated airframe and power plant mechanic may sign off and approve the return to service following what inspection?
Airframe Inspection.a: 100-hour inspection

Airframe Inspection.q: In what regulation is the definition of airworthy found?
Airframe Inspection.a: The definition of airworthy is found in 14 CFR Part 3.

Airframe Inspection.q: What does the FAA issue when an unsafe condition exists in a product and the condition is likely to exist or develop in other products of the same type design?
Airframe Inspection.a: The FAA issues airworthiness directives.

Airframe Inspection.q: What is the person performing the inspection required to do regarding items that are permitted to be inoperative under Part 91.213.D.2?
Airframe Inspection.a: If the person performing any inspection finds discrepancies, that person must give the owner or lessee a signed and dated list of those discrepancies.

Airframe Inspection.q: Under what conditions may a mechanic perform an annual inspection?
Airframe Inspection.a: The mechanic must hold an inspection authorization.

Airframe Inspection.q: Why does the FAA issue airworthiness directives?
Airframe Inspection.a: When an unsafe condition exists in a product and the condition is likely to exist or develop in other products of the same type design.

Airframe Inspection.q: What is the timeframe within which an annual inspection must be performed if such an inspection is required?
Airframe Inspection.a: Within 12 calendar months.

Airframe Inspection.q: Under what conditions may a checklist be used as a guide for a 100-hour inspection?
Airframe Inspection.a: The checklist must meet the minimum standards outlined in 14 CFR Part 43, Appendix D.

Airframe Inspection.q: How may an unsafe condition be addressed, in a way, other than that set out in an airworthiness directive?
Airframe Inspection.a: Anyone may propose to the FAA an alternative method of compliance or a change in the compliance time, if it provides an acceptable level of safety.

Airframe Inspection.q: May an aircraft that is overdue for an annual inspection be flown to a place where the inspection can be performed?
Airframe Inspection.a: Yes, provided a special flight permit has been obtained from the FAA.

Airframe Inspection.q: How long must the aircraft owner retain the records for a 100-hour inspection?
Airframe Inspection.a: They shall be retained until the work is repeated or superseded by other work, or for one year after the work is performed.

Airframe Inspection.q: Aircraft under what type of inspection program do not require a 100-hour inspection, even if operated for compensation or hire?
Airframe Inspection.a: Aircraft operated under a progressive inspection program.

Airframe Inspection.q: What should be done if the aircraft's registration is not current?
Airframe Inspection.a: The aircraft's owner should be notified.

Airframe Inspection.q: In order for an individual person to conduct a 100-hour inspection and approve the return to service, that person must have what type of certificate?
Airframe Inspection.a: A mechanic's certificate with both airframe and power plant ratings.

Airframe Inspection.q: How can an unsafe condition be addressed in a way other than that set out in an airworthiness directive?
Airframe Inspection.a: An alternative method of compliance can be requested.

Airframe Inspection.q: An aircraft that is required by 14 CFR Part 91-409 to have a 100-hour inspection. Is it legal to fly that airplane beyond the inspection requirement, and if so, for how long?
Airframe Inspection.a: Yes, up to 10 hours, but only to reach the location where the inspection is to be conducted.

Airframe Inspection.q: An aircraft is flown 4 hours over the 100-hour inspection limit to reach your maintenance facility. How should you account for those 4 hours overflown?
Airframe Inspection.a: The 4 hours are included in the next 100 hours time in service period.

Airframe Inspection.q: What types of operations require that aircraft operating under 14 CFR must undergo 100-hour inspections?
Airframe Inspection.a: Aircraft operated for compensation or hire, or used for flight instruction when provided by the flight instructor.

Airframe Inspection.q: It is important that an inspection for corrosion includes what sides of an aircraft structure?
Airframe Inspection.a: Both the outside and inside of the aircraft structure.

Airframe Inspection.q: What may result if Appendix D of Part 43 is not referenced when developing your own checklist to perform a 100-hour or annual inspection?
Airframe Inspection.a: The inspection may miss required items or contain unnecessary items.

Airframe Inspection.q: A life-limited part is a part with a mandatory replacement limit specified in the aircraft's type design, instructions for continued airworthiness, or what other document?
Airframe Inspection.a: The Maintenance Manual.

Airframe Inspection.q: An aircraft under a progressive inspection program is operated for compensation or hire. Does it need a 100-hour inspection?
Airframe Inspection.a: No, progressive inspections replace 100-hour inspections.

Airframe Inspection.q: Who is authorized to supervise a progressive inspection?
Airframe Inspection.a: A certificated mechanic with airframe and power plant ratings holding an inspection authorization, a certified repair station, or the aircraft manufacturer.

Airframe Inspection.q: What can be missed if only a visual inspection is performed to major structural members?
Airframe Inspection.a: Suspected internal cracks or damage.

Airframe Inspection.q: When may a non-certificated person work under a certificated mechanic to perform a 100-hour inspection?
Airframe Inspection.a: Never. Part 43 does not authorize inspections by a non-certificated person.

Airframe Inspection.q: When a progressive inspection program is discontinued, at what time is the next 100-hour inspection due?
Airframe Inspection.a: 100 hours after the last complete inspection.

Airframe Inspection.q: An owner desires to use a progressive inspection program. To whom does the owner apply for authorization?
Airframe Inspection.a: The FAA Flight Standards District Office (FSDO) having jurisdiction.

Airframe Inspection.q: Is an engine run-up required as part of a 100-hour inspection on a turbine-powered aircraft?
Airframe Inspection.a: Yes.

Airframe Inspection.q: Where would you locate the required items that must be recorded in the aircraft maintenance records following the completion of a 100-hour inspection?
Airframe Inspection.a: 14 CFR Part 43.11

Airframe Inspection.q: Where might you locate a sample of the recommended statement for recording the approval for return to service of an aircraft following the completion of an annual or 100-hour inspection?
Airframe Inspection.a: 14 CFR Part 43.11

Airframe Inspection.q: Where would you look to find approved information for work on an aircraft electrical system?
Airframe Inspection.a: In the manufacturer maintenance manual or other instructions for continued airworthiness.

Airframe Inspection.q: What are the limitations placed on mechanics with regard to propellers?
Airframe Inspection.a: Mechanics may not perform major repairs to or alterations of propellers.

Airframe Inspection.q: After equipment is installed in accordance with the STC, who must approve the return to service?
Airframe Inspection.a: An airframe and power plant mechanic who also holds an inspection authorization.

Airframe Inspection.q: If required, what inspection must be performed every 12 calendar months?
Airframe Inspection.a: An annual inspection.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What are the basic components of a radio communication system?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Microphone, transmitter, transmitting and receiving antennas, receiver, and speaker or headphones.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What frequency range is used by most modern voice communication systems?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: VHF – Very High Frequency.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What does the term ADF stand for?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Automatic Direction Finder.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What does the acronym ADS-B stand for?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Automatic Dependent Surveillance Broadcast.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What is the function of VOR equipment?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Navigation.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What rating must a mechanic have to return to service the adjustment of the flight control travel?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: A mechanic must have an airframe rating.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: Name the components of a typical airborne VOR system.
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Receiver, antennas, frequency selector, and visual display indicator.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What does DME stand for?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Distance Measuring Equipment.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: At what intervals must an ATC transponder system be certified?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Every 24 calendar months.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What are the two antennas usually required for an ILS system?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Localizer and glideslope antennas (on different frequencies).

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: Where can you find the travel limits of the flight controls for a specific aircraft?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Aircraft specifications, type certificate data sheet, or the aircraft maintenance manual.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What information does the glideslope beam provide?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Vertical guidance to maintain the correct descent angle.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What is the power source for an ELT?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: An internal battery.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What is the general resistance requirement for static discharge wicks?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Resistance from the mount to the airframe should not exceed 0.1 ohm.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: Describe one method of monitoring the signal output during an ELT test.
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Tune a communications receiver to 121.5 MHz.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: How would you determine if the batteries in an ELT are due for recharge or replacement?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: By checking the expiration date on the battery or in the aircraft maintenance records.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What is the general purpose of static discharge wicks?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: To reduce radio receiver interference.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: At what time of day is a test of an installed ELT permitted?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: During the first five minutes after the hour and for no more than three sweeps.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: Where on the aircraft would you expect to find a DME antenna?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Generally on the bottom of the fuselage in a location not blocked by the wing during turns.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: Describe the shape and location of a transponder antenna.
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Either a short blade or a short wire with a ball end, located on the bottom of the fuselage.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What is the rule of thumb minimum distance between antennas?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: 36 inches minimum.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: Name the basic components of an autopilot system.
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Gyros, servos, controller, and amplifier or computer.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What are the sensing elements of an autopilot system?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Attitude gyros, directional gyros, turning gyros, and altitude sensor.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: Where should a GPS antenna be located?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: On the top side of the aircraft.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What is the function of the servos in an autopilot system?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: To actuate the flight controls.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What is the purpose of the bonding jumper from a radio shock mount to the airframe?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: To provide a low impedance ground return.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What regulation covers testing of ELTs?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: 14 CFR Part 91.207.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: When testing a non-406 MHz ELT, how long should the test last?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: No longer than three audible sweeps.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: Describe the procedures used to route coaxial cable from an antenna to a receiver.
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Support every 24 inches, bends at least 10× cable diameter, and route separately from other wires.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What are the functions of the amplifier in the autopilot system?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: To strengthen the signal for processing and for use by servos.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: Similar to light waves, radio waves are what type of wave?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Electromagnetic waves.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What is a radio system component called that contains both a transmitter and receiver in one unit?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Transceiver.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What is the name of the radio system component used to transmit and receive radio frequency waves?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Antenna.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What is used in an interphone or intercom system to transmit voice?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Microphone.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What are the functions of the computer in an autopilot system?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Interpret sensing element data, integrate commands and navigation inputs, and send signals to output elements.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: When should the ELT batteries be recharged or replaced?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: When 50% of useful life has expired, after one cumulative hour of use, or at the expiration date set by the manufacturer.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: If the batteries are no longer available for an ELT manufactured under TSO C91, what must be done?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: The ELT must be replaced.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What information does a glideslope radio beam provide?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Vertical guidance for the correct descent angle.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What two radio bands are used in an ILS system?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: VHF and UHF.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What are the two primary radio navigation systems in the ILS system?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Localizer and glideslope.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What does the acronym TCAS stand for?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Traffic Collision Avoidance System.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What function does the aircraft ground proximity warning system provide?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Warns pilots of potentially dangerous proximity to terrain.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: An attitude gyro is classified in what element of the autopilot system?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Sensing element.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What are the sensing element components in an autopilot system?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Attitude gyro, directional gyros, turn coordinator, and altitude control.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What autopilot element consists of attitude and directional gyros, turn coordinator, and altitude control?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Sensing elements.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What system is comprised of sensing elements, gyros, servos, command elements, flight controller, computer, and amplifier?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Autopilot system.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What is the name of the system found on rotorcraft that helps stabilize in flight and hover?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Stability Augmentation System.

Communications and Navigation Systems.q: What altimeter system measures the height of the aircraft above the terrain and is used for landing decision height?
Communications and Navigation Systems.a: Radio (Radar) Altimeter System.

Environmental Systems.q: Is there any special requirement as to the type of oxygen used in an aircraft system, and if so, what is it?
Environmental Systems.a: Only aviators breathing oxygen should be used.

Environmental Systems.q: Describe the operating principle of a continuous flow oxygen system.
Environmental Systems.a: High pressure oxygen flows from the storage cylinder to a pressure regulator, where its pressure is reduced and then to the mask outlets, whenever the system is turned on.

Environmental Systems.q: Describe the operating principle of a pressure demand oxygen system.
Environmental Systems.a: Pressure-demand systems allow oxygen to flow from the cylinder to the regulator and then to the mask only when the user inhales. Pressure-demand systems provide oxygen to the mask at higher than atmospheric pressure when used at extremely high altitudes, forcing oxygen into the user's lungs.

Environmental Systems.q: Which gases should be used to purge oxygen lines of moisture?
Environmental Systems.a: Oxygen, dry air, or dry nitrogen.

Environmental Systems.q: In a combustion heater, what ensures that the correct quantity and pressure of air are sent into the combustion air chamber?
Environmental Systems.a: A blower ensures that the correct quantity and pressure of air are sent into the combustion air chamber.

Environmental Systems.q: What happens when the overheat switch is activated in the combustion heater?
Environmental Systems.a: When the overheat switch activates, a light is illuminated in the cockpit and the heater cannot be restarted until maintenance determines the cause.

Environmental Systems.q: Leak detection methods can be employed in a vapor cycle system that has no refrigerant. The system needs to be partially charged with how much refrigerant?
Environmental Systems.a: About 50 psi of refrigerant in the high and low sides should be sufficient for a leak check.

Environmental Systems.q: What action must be taken when an oxygen system has been opened to the atmosphere?
Environmental Systems.a: The system must be purged of any moisture.

Environmental Systems.q: Why is the use of an air cycle system impractical for conditioning cabin air on a normally aspirated reciprocating engine aircraft?
Environmental Systems.a: There is no bleed air source on a normally aspirated reciprocating engine aircraft.

Environmental Systems.q: Describe the safety precautions that should be observed when servicing oxygen systems.
Environmental Systems.a: Avoid all contact with petroleum-based oil or grease. Don’t smoke. Keep everything very clean. Service systems outdoors if at all possible. And keep the caps on the bottles to protect the valves.

Environmental Systems.q: Aircraft cabin pressurization can be controlled by two different modes of operation. What are they and how do they work?
Environmental Systems.a: The first is the isobaric mode, which maintains cabin altitude at a pressure selected by the crew. The second is the constant differential mode, which controls cabin pressure to maintain a constant pressure difference between the cabin and ambient air pressure.

Environmental Systems.q: When using an oxygen filler cart to service an aircraft’s oxygen system, what is the minimum cylinder pressure allowable for the service cart cylinders?
Environmental Systems.a: 50 psi of oxygen pressure.

Environmental Systems.q: What is a roots blower?
Environmental Systems.a: A type of engine-driven compressor.

Environmental Systems.q: What is the most common source of pressurized air used by turbine-powered aircraft for pressurization?
Environmental Systems.a: Engine bleed air.

Environmental Systems.q: What are the three typical sources of air used to pressurize reciprocating aircraft?
Environmental Systems.a: Supercharger, turbocharger, and engine-driven compressor.

Environmental Systems.q: Name two different types of independent cabin air compressor.
Environmental Systems.a: Positive displacement and centrifugal.

Environmental Systems.q: How do non-air-conditioned aircraft cool the avionics equipment?
Environmental Systems.a: A blower or scooped ram air.

Environmental Systems.q: At what pressure are oxygen cylinders considered empty?
Environmental Systems.a: Below 50 psi.

Environmental Systems.q: Why is it allowable to fill an oxygen cylinder to a higher pressure than the standard maximum when it is hot?
Environmental Systems.a: Because at altitude the temperature and pressure of the oxygen can decrease significantly.

Environmental Systems.q: What device provides the principal means of controlling cabin pressure?
Environmental Systems.a: The outflow valve.

Environmental Systems.q: What unit regulates the position of the outflow valve?
Environmental Systems.a: The cabin pressure controller.

Environmental Systems.q: What is an oxygen safe leak check fluid?
Environmental Systems.a: A soapy liquid, free from elements that might react with pure oxygen or contaminate the system.

Environmental Systems.q: Other than an electric heater, what is a common method of heating the cabin of a single-engine reciprocating aircraft?
Environmental Systems.a: An exhaust shroud heating system.

Environmental Systems.q: Name several methods used on reciprocating engine aircraft for providing heated cabin air.
Environmental Systems.a: Exhaust shroud heat exchangers, electric heaters, and combustion heaters (multi-engine).

Environmental Systems.q: What is the function of ventilating air in a combustion heater?
Environmental Systems.a: It transports the heat into the cabin and prevents combustion gases from entering if the chamber cracks.

Environmental Systems.q: What are sources of ventilating air in a combustion heater?
Environmental Systems.a: Ram air in flight, a blower on the ground, or a compressor in pressurized aircraft.

Environmental Systems.q: Name the basic components of an air cycle cooling system.
Environmental Systems.a: Compressor, expansion turbine, heat exchangers, and various valves.

Environmental Systems.q: How is ventilating air supplied to the combustion heater on the ground?
Environmental Systems.a: A blower fan.

Environmental Systems.q: Describe the operating principles that allow an air cycle system to produce cool air.
Environmental Systems.a: Hot bleed air is cooled in the primary heat exchanger, compressed, cooled again in the secondary exchanger, then expanded across the turbine where energy is extracted and pressure reduced, producing a large temperature drop.

Environmental Systems.q: What system allows oxygen to flow from a charged cylinder to a pressure-demand regulator, only when demanded?
Environmental Systems.a: Pressure-demand oxygen system.

Environmental Systems.q: What type of crew oxygen system allows oxygen to flow from a cylinder through a high-pressure line to a reducing valve and to mask outlets?
Environmental Systems.a: Continuous-flow oxygen system.

Environmental Systems.q: What is the purpose of ventilating air in a combustion heater?
Environmental Systems.a: It is the air used to warm the cabin.

Environmental Systems.q: An oxygen system that is open must be initially purged with nitrogen or dry air, but finally purged with what gas?
Environmental Systems.a: Oxygen.

Environmental Systems.q: Describe the operating principles of a water separator.
Environmental Systems.a: Cool moist air is swirled so water is separated by centrifugal force, captured by a sock, and drained.

Environmental Systems.q: An aircraft air conditioning system that uses bleed air from a turbine engine is what type?
Environmental Systems.a: Air cycle cooling system.

Environmental Systems.q: What part of the pressurization system maintains the desired pressure level in the cabin?
Environmental Systems.a: The outflow valve.

Environmental Systems.q: A compressor, condenser, receiver-dryer, expansion valve, and evaporator are parts of what environmental system?
Environmental Systems.a: Vapor cycle cooling system.

Environmental Systems.q: What closed system uses refrigerant to remove heat from the cabin?
Environmental Systems.a: Vapor cycle cooling system.

Environmental Systems.q: What component in the pressurization system controls the outflow valve?
Environmental Systems.a: The cabin pressure controller.

Environmental Systems.q: What is vapor cycle cooling?
Environmental Systems.a: Freon cooling.

Environmental Systems.q: What causes a temperature drop in an air cycle cooling system?
Environmental Systems.a: Expansion of air at the turbine outlet.

Environmental Systems.q: What component reduces hot bleed air temperature before entering the air cycle machine?
Environmental Systems.a: Primary heat exchanger.

Environmental Systems.q: In what way is a vapor cycle cooling system different from an air cycle system?
Environmental Systems.a: Vapor cycle systems use refrigerant liquid, usually Freon.

Environmental Systems.q: What component in an air cycle cooling system swirls moist air to collect and drain water droplets?
Environmental Systems.a: Water separator.

Environmental Systems.q: Why is oil added to refrigerant in a vapor cycle system?
Environmental Systems.a: To lubricate the compressor.

Fire Protection.q: Briefly describe the components of a thermal switch fire detection system.
Fire Protection.a: Temperature-sensitive switches that complete a circuit at a specific temperature and trigger a warning

Fire Protection.q: How are thermal switch units wired?
Fire Protection.a: Parallel with each other and in series with the light

Fire Protection.q: How are Fenwal sensing elements wired?
Fire Protection.a: Connected in series with the control unit

Fire Protection.q: When testing a continuous-loop fire detection system, how do you know that the sensing loop is unbroken and that the control circuits function correctly?
Fire Protection.a: The fire alarm will activate

Fire Protection.q: How are the detector units in a double-loop Fenwal system wired?
Fire Protection.a: In parallel between two complete loops of wiring

Fire Protection.q: In what way is a thermocouple fire warning system different from a thermal switch system?
Fire Protection.a: Thermocouple systems are sensitive to the rate of temperature change, whereas thermal switch systems respond to a specific temperature value

Fire Protection.q: Briefly describe the components of a Kidde continuous-loop fire detection system.
Fire Protection.a: A Kidde fire loop element consists of two wires embedded in an Inconel tube filled with a thermistor core material

Fire Protection.q: What indication may be caused by chafing, kinks, and sharp bends in sensing elements of a fire detection system?
Fire Protection.a: False fire warning

Fire Protection.q: Describe the mechanism by which a photoelectric smoke detector generates a warning of a possible fire.
Fire Protection.a: Smoke particles refract light in the detector unit, causing the photoelectric cell to conduct electricity and trigger an alarm

Fire Protection.q: How can the operation of a photoelectric smoke detector be tested?
Fire Protection.a: By actuating a test switch

Fire Protection.q: Halogenated hydrocarbons (Halon), carbon dioxide (CO₂), dry powders, and water are all types of materials used for what purpose?
Fire Protection.a: They are all types of fire extinguishing agents

Fire Protection.q: Name two types of smoke detection systems used in aircraft.
Fire Protection.a: Light refraction (photoelectric) types and ionization types

Fire Protection.q: What are the usual locations where a carbon monoxide detector would be installed?
Fire Protection.a: In the aircraft cockpit or cabin

Fire Protection.q: How does a carbon monoxide detector indicate the presence of carbon monoxide?
Fire Protection.a: The spot on the detector turns color (gray, black, or green). More advanced detectors may also use an alarm and light

Fire Protection.q: What are the possible effects of kinks and sharp bends in the sensing element(s) of a fire warning system?
Fire Protection.a: False fire warnings caused by short circuits

Fire Protection.q: How is a Freon container checked for proper pressure?
Fire Protection.a: A chart must be used to determine the max and min gauge pressure readings based on ambient temperature

Fire Protection.q: Name two types of fire extinguishing systems.
Fire Protection.a: Conventional system and High Rate of Discharge (HRD) system

Fire Protection.q: To detect fires or overheat conditions, detectors are placed in various zones. Name the different means of fire detection.
Fire Protection.a: Rate-of-temperature-rise detectors, overheat detectors, flame detectors, and visual detection

Fire Protection.q: Why is a temperature-pressure chart used when checking a fire extinguisher container pressure?
Fire Protection.a: Ambient temperature affects container pressure; the chart shows the acceptable min and max gauge readings

Fire Protection.q: Name the extinguishing agent most commonly used by the conventional fire extinguishing system.
Fire Protection.a: Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)

Fire Protection.q: Name the extinguishing agent most commonly used by high-rate discharge extinguishing systems.
Fire Protection.a: Halon (Halogenated Hydrocarbon)

Fire Protection.q: The integrity of the continuous-loop fire detection system may be tested by actuating a test switch. What does this simulate?
Fire Protection.a: The sensing element resistance change due to a fire

Fire Protection.q: A fire detection system should signal the presence of a fire. Name the three detector system types.
Fire Protection.a: Thermal switch, thermocouple, and continuous loop system

Fire Protection.q: Large turbine-engine aircraft fire protection systems use multiple detection methods. Name them.
