Fire Protection.a: Smoke detectors, rate-of-temp-rise detectors, radiation sensing detectors, overheat detectors, carbon monoxide detectors, optical detectors, combustible mixture detectors, visible observation

Fire Protection.q: What are the six zones in an aircraft that have fixed fire detection and/or fire extinguisher systems?
Fire Protection.a: Engines and APU, Cargo compartments, Lavatories, Electronic bays, Wheel wells, and Bleed air ducts

Fire Protection.q: Why does turbine bleed air used for cabin heating pose no threat of carbon monoxide poisoning?
Fire Protection.a: The bleed air is tapped upstream of the combustion chamber

Fire Protection.q: How are Freon bottles protected from high temperature, and how can you tell if one discharged due to this?
Fire Protection.a: A thermal fuse releases the agent overboard, blowing out a red indicator disk (thermal discharge)

Fire Protection.q: What condition is indicated if the yellow disk is missing in a fire extinguisher system?
Fire Protection.a: The bottle was discharged by the pilot or crew

Fire Protection.q: Name the types of continuous-loop fire detection systems.
Fire Protection.a: Kidde, Fenwal, and Lingberg systems

Fire Protection.q: What materials are involved in a Class B fire?
Fire Protection.a: Flammable liquids/gases, petroleum oils, greases, solvents, and alcohols

Fire Protection.q: Describe the mechanism by which a Freon bottle is discharged.
Fire Protection.a: An electrical signal fires an explosive squib, which ruptures a fragile disk

Fire Protection.q: How is the service life of a discharge cartridge calculated?
Fire Protection.a: From the manufacturer’s date stamped on the cartridge

Fire Protection.q: What procedure is used to check a fire extinguisher container for pressure?
Fire Protection.a: A chart is used to determine min and max gauge readings based on temperature

Fire Protection.q: How many classes of fire are considered possible to occur on aircraft?
Fire Protection.a: Four

Flight Controls.q: What is the function of a vortex generator?
Flight Controls.a: It is designed to delay or prevent separation of the boundary layer on the upper surface of a wing.

Flight Controls.q: What are the three axes of an aircraft?
Flight Controls.a: Longitudinal, lateral, and vertical.

Flight Controls.q: What are the three primary flight controls?
Flight Controls.a: The ailerons, elevator, and rudder.

Flight Controls.q: What are the secondary flight controls and describe their general purpose?
Flight Controls.a: Secondary flight controls consist of trim tabs such as balance tabs, antiservo tabs, and spring tabs. Their function is to assist the pilot in moving the controls and to trim the aircraft to fly hands-off.

Flight Controls.q: Name several types of auxiliary flight controls and describe their general purposes.
Flight Controls.a: Auxiliary flight controls include high-lift devices used during low-speed flight, such as leading and trailing edge flaps, slats, slots, and speed brakes.

Flight Controls.q: What is a servo trim tab?
Flight Controls.a: A secondary control, positioned by cockpit movement, designed to create aerodynamic forces to assist in moving a control surface.

Flight Controls.q: What is a spring tab?
Flight Controls.a: A secondary control designed to aid movement of a primary control at high speeds when forces become too high.

Flight Controls.q: What is a balance tab?
Flight Controls.a: A secondary control designed to create aerodynamic forces to assist in moving a control surface, positioned by a control rod connected to the fixed surface.

Flight Controls.q: What are the four most common types of high-lift devices?
Flight Controls.a: Leading and trailing edge flaps, slats, and slots.

Flight Controls.q: What tools are used to check control surface travel?
Flight Controls.a: Universal propeller protractor, special control surface protractor, contour templates, rigging fixtures, and rulers.

Flight Controls.q: What are the mechanical methods by which flight control systems may be actuated?
Flight Controls.a: Cables, push-pull rods, and torque tubes.

Flight Controls.q: What are critical fatigue areas for cables?
Flight Controls.a: Anywhere cables run over, under, or around pulleys, sleeves, or fairleads; or within one foot of a swaged fitting.

Flight Controls.q: What is a cable fairlead?
Flight Controls.a: A device to prevent a cable from rubbing on the aircraft structure.

Flight Controls.q: What are the most likely places for a control cable to wear or break?
Flight Controls.a: Where cables pass over pulleys or through fairleads.

Flight Controls.q: What information is required before a cable rigging chart can be used?
Flight Controls.a: The ambient temperature and cable size.

Flight Controls.q: Why is it not permissible to solder cable ends to prevent fraying?
Flight Controls.a: Solder increases the tendency of the cable to pull out of the terminal.

Flight Controls.q: What is used to control cable tension when aluminum structure and steel cables expand differently?
Flight Controls.a: Cable tension regulators.

Flight Controls.q: What are two of the most common methods of balancing or rebalancing control surfaces?
Flight Controls.a: Calculation method, scale method, and balance beam method.

Flight Controls.q: When must a control surface be rebalanced?
Flight Controls.a: Any time repairs add weight aft of the hinge center line.

Flight Controls.q: What are the two ways a flight control may be out of static balance?
Flight Controls.a: Underbalance and overbalance.

Flight Controls.q: What is the function of a cable tension regulator?
Flight Controls.a: Automatically adjusts cable tension to compensate for structural expansion/contraction.

Flight Controls.q: What materials are used to fabricate aircraft control cables?
Flight Controls.a: Carbon steel or stainless steel.

Flight Controls.q: What is the function of the rotorcraft collective pitch control?
Flight Controls.a: Causes all rotor blades to change pitch angle by the same amount, increasing or decreasing lift.

Flight Controls.q: What type of flexible cable is used for primary control systems with frequent pulley use?
Flight Controls.a: 7×19 extra flexible carbon steel or stainless steel.

Flight Controls.q: What type of medium flexible cable is used for trim tab, engine, and indicator controls?
Flight Controls.a: 7×7 medium flexible carbon steel or stainless steel.

Flight Controls.q: What is built into an analog tensiometer for reading cable tension when the dial is hard to see?
Flight Controls.a: A pointer lock.

Flight Controls.q: What unit compensates for helicopter main rotor torque?
Flight Controls.a: An auxiliary or tail rotor.

Flight Controls.q: What causes primary flight control surface flutter?
Flight Controls.a: An unbalanced control surface.

Flight Controls.q: What is the function of the rotorcraft cyclic pitch control?
Flight Controls.a: Tilts the rotor disk by changing each blade’s pitch angle during rotation, moving the helicopter in the tilt direction.

Flight Controls.q: What permits the pilot to change tail rotor thrust to neutralize torque effect?
Flight Controls.a: Foot pedals in the flight deck.

Flight Controls.q: What mechanism is most commonly used to compensate for main rotor torque in a helicopter?
Flight Controls.a: Tail or anti-torque rotor.

Flight Controls.q: How is tail rotor thrust controlled?
Flight Controls.a: By moving the foot pedals.

Flight Controls.q: On a push-pull tube, where would you look for elongated holes?
Flight Controls.a: At the riveted joints of push-pull rods.

Flight Controls.q: When inspecting the rod end of a push-pull tube, where do cracks usually begin?
Flight Controls.a: At the thread end near the bearing and adjacent to/under the jam nut.

Flight Controls.q: Why should control surfaces be locked when the aircraft is parked?
Flight Controls.a: To prevent damage from the wind.

Flight Controls.q: Torque tubes are used when what type of motion is needed?
Flight Controls.a: Angular, twisting, or rotational motion.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.q: What are seals used for phosphate-ester based hydraulic fluids made from?
Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: Seals for phosphate-ester based fluids are made from butyl rubber or

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.q: type fluid?
Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: If the wrong fluid is used, immediately drain and flush the system

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.q: What colors denote mineral base and phosphate-ester based hydraulic fluids?
Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: Mineral based fluids are red and phosphate-ester based fluids are

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.q: vinyl compounds to phosphate-ester based hydraulic fluid?
Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: The materials will be damaged and/or softened or the paint will

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.q: Name several sources used to develop hydraulic pressure.
Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: Hand pumps, engine-driven pumps, and electrically driven pumps.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.q: What are the three main classes of seals?
Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: Seals are divided into three main classes: packings, gaskets,

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.q: when the actuating mechanism are idle?
Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: An open center hydraulic system.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.q: filter and why?
Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: A face shield or other protective eye wear and protective clothing

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.q: to add fluid to the reservoir in a non-pressurized aircraft?
Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: This method reduces the chances of contamination of the

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.q: allow unfiltered fluid to circulate in the system?
Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: A clogged filter element.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.q: Describe several methods of pressurizing a hydraulic reservoir?
Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: Engine bleed air, system pressure acting on a small piston in

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.q: of an emergency?
Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: An (emergency system) accumulator would be used.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.q: What are the ports in a fluid-pressurized reservoir?
Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: The reservoir has five ports: pump suction, return,

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.q: pressurized?
Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: Pressurizing assures a positive flow of fluid to the pump

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.q: pump seizes or is overloaded?

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: Pressure relief valves limit the maximum pressure

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.q: What are two types of engine-driven hydraulic pumps?
Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: Constant-delivery and variable-delivery.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.q: MIL-H- 83282 hydraulic fluids?
Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: MIL-H-83282 is more flame retardant.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.q: Engine-driven pumps deliver the output fluid flow in what two forms?
Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: As constant volume or a variable volume.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.q: based fluids) in place of MIL_H_5606 (mineral based fluids)?
Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: MIL-H-83282 has a high viscosity at low temperatures.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.q: Describe the operation of a gear type pump.
Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: Two meshed gears, driven by a power source, rotate

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.q: Describe the purpose of a pressure regulator.
Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: Pressure regulators manage the output of the pump

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.q: the pump output during periods of high demand?
Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: An accumulator.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.q: pressure and potential damage to the system?
Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: The pressure relief valve.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.q: What are the three different types of accumulators?
Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: Piston, bladder, and diaphragm.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: Release all air pressure or preload.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.q: pressure in what system component?

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: Brakes; opening and closing doors; driving

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: Storage bottles, engine bleed air, engine-

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.q: What is the function of a restrictor in a pneumatic system?
Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: To control the rate of airflow in the system.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.q: when the filter is clogged?
Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.a: It is the filter bypass valve.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: What controls the temperature in a thermal anti-icing system?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: Hot and cold air are mixed.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: What are the sources of heat for thermal anti-icing systems?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: Bleed air from the compressor section of a turbine engine, engine exhaust heat exchangers, and ram air heated by combustion heaters.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: What prevents overheating of the leading edges of a thermal anti-ice system operated by engine bleed air?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: Overheat sensors signal the anti-icing valves to close and shut off the hot air.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: What happens to the hot air used by a thermal anti-ice system after it has heated the leading edge surface?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: The air is dumped or exhausted overboard.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: Why is it necessary to provide overheat protection for anti-icing systems that use turbine engine bleed air?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: Because the air is hot enough to cause damage to the aircraft structure.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: What are the effects of arcing on an electrically heated windshield?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: Arcing causes localized overheating and damage to the windshield.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: Can the operation of an electrically heated pitot tube be checked with the aircraft's ammeter? If so, how?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: Yes, turn the pitot heat on and observe the deflection of the ammeter needle.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: Describe several potential problems associated with electrically heated windshields.
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: Arcing, delamination, scratches, shorts, opens, and discoloration.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: Why do some pneumatic de-icer boot systems have an electrically operated timer?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: To automatically cycle the boots, provide the proper rest time, and then recycle the boots.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: What are the two common methods of inflating pneumatic de-icer boots?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: Bleed air from a turbine engine or the exhaust from an engine-driven vacuum pump.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: What procedure is used to hold de-icer boots flat with the airfoil surface during flight?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: Suction is applied to the boots.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: Why do some de-icer boot systems incorporate an oil separator?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: If a wet pump system is used, the oil must be removed before it reaches the boots because oil damages the rubber.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: What methods are used to attach a de-icer boot to the leading edges of the wing and tail surfaces?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: Adhesives, fairing strips and screws, or a combination of both.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: What important steps should be taken prior to making a cold patch repair to a de-icer boot?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: The de-icer boot must be cleaned thoroughly and the surface roughened. Always consult the manufacturer's service manual and follow the repair instructions.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: What are two inherent problems associated with windshield wipers when operated in flight?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: Aerodynamic forces reduce blade pressure, and the wipers fail to achieve fast enough oscillations.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: Describe the methods commonly used to remove rain from a windshield.
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: Windshield wipers, a blast of air, chemical rain repellent, and hydrophobic surface seal coating.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: What power sources are used to operate windshield wipers?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: Electricity or hydraulic pressure.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: Describe the operation of a pneumatic rain removal system.
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: A high-velocity air blast prevents the rain from reaching the surface of the windshield.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: Why should rain repellent not be sprayed on a dry windshield?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: It smears, causes streaks that reduce visibility, and is hard to remove when dry.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: Where would you find information on the acceptable limits of delamination of a windshield?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: Manufacturer's Maintenance Manual or Windshield Manufacturer's Publications.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: What type of hazards does ice or frost forming on an aircraft create?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: Malformation of the airfoil that decreases lift, additional weight, and unequal ice formation that can unbalance the aircraft and make it hard to control.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: How is anti-icing equipment used?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: It is turned on before entering icing conditions and is designed to prevent ice from forming.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: What areas or parts of an aircraft are anti-iced?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: Windshields, windows, engine air inlets, pitot and static air data sensors, propeller blade leading edges and spinners, carburetors, lavatory drains, and potable water lines.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: What is the source for inflating pneumatic de-icer boots on aircraft with reciprocating engines?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: An engine-driven air pump.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: On a general aviation aircraft, what holds the deflated de-icer boots tightly against the surface of an airfoil?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: Suction from vacuum.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: Why is it important that the timer control unit in a pneumatic de-icer boot system operate correctly?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: It controls the operating sequence and time intervals.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: What three steps must be taken to prepare a de-icer boot for a cold patch repair?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: The de-icer boot must be deflated and tension relieved, the surface cleaned, and the boot buffed/roughened.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: What are the different types of heating elements that are used on pitot tubes?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: Internal coil wires, externally wrapped blankets or tapes, conductive films, or heated gaskets.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: Since icing can degrade vision, how are the windshields of transport category aircraft anti-iced?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: By the use of thermal electric anti-icing systems.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: What three aircraft conditions are used by some transport aircraft to automatically energize or de-energize probe anti-ice heating circuits?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: Aircraft speed, whether the aircraft is on the ground or in flight, and whether the engines are running.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: What sensors or probes most commonly use thermal electric anti-icing?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: Air data probes, total air temperature probes, angle of attack probes, ice detectors, and engine P2T2 sensors.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: What is used to resurface de-icer boots?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: Black, conductive neoprene cement.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: What is recommended to clean grease and oil from de-icer boots?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: A cleaning agent such as naphtha, followed by soap and water scrubbing.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: How is the temperature regulated in a thermal pneumatic anti-icing system?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: By mixing the heated air with ambient cold air.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: What is the most common servicing operation that can cause damage to wing de-icer boots?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: Dragging the fueling hose across de-icer boots during refueling.

Ice and Rain Control Systems.q: What is generally recommended if a de-ice boot shows signs of weather cracking?
Ice and Rain Control Systems.a: Apply a coat of conductive cement.

Landing Gear.q: What is the purpose of a torque link on an oleo strut?
Landing Gear.a: To keep the wheel in alignment and prevent the piston from coming out of the cylinder.

Landing Gear.q: A landing gear shock strut should be inflated with what gas?
Landing Gear.a: Nitrogen or dry air.

Landing Gear.q: What does the tire bead do?
Landing Gear.a: It anchors the tire carcass and provides a dimensioned, firm mounting surface for the tire on the wheel rim.

Landing Gear.q: What landing gear warnings are provided for retractable landing gear systems?
Landing Gear.a: A horn or other oral device and a red warning light.

Landing Gear.q: What are possible causes that make brakes drag?
Landing Gear.a: Linings not retracting from the brake disc, faulty return mechanism, or warped disc from overheating.

Landing Gear.q: When the brakes show signs of overheating, what must be done?
Landing Gear.a: Inspect the brakes per the manufacturer’s maintenance manual.

Landing Gear.q: When the landing gear hydraulic selector valve is in the down position, what stops the pump motor?
Landing Gear.a: When all three gear are fully down and locked.

Landing Gear.q: When does brake glazing occur?
Landing Gear.a: When a disc or part of a disc is overheated.

Landing Gear.q: What is the unique feature of the Cleveland Brake design?
Landing Gear.a: The brake linings can be changed without jacking the aircraft or removing the wheel.

Landing Gear.q: What can be used to measure lining wear on a Cleveland Brake?
Landing Gear.a: A number 40 twist drill bit.

Landing Gear.q: What are tire plies and what do they do?
Landing Gear.a: Reinforcing fabric layers encased in rubber that provide tire strength.

Landing Gear.q: What controls fluid flow between chambers in a shock strut?
Landing Gear.a: A metering pin, orifice, or metering tube.

Landing Gear.q: During what test should the landing gear be checked for proper operation?
Landing Gear.a: Landing gear retraction test (gear swing).

Landing Gear.q: What effect does insufficient fluid/air have on strut compression stroke?
Landing Gear.a: Strut may bottom out, transferring impact directly to the airframe.

Landing Gear.q: What should be checked during inspection of exposed piston section?
Landing Gear.a: Cleanliness, damage, and proper extension.

Landing Gear.q: What prevents air leaks between split wheel halves?
Landing Gear.a: An O-ring.

Landing Gear.q: Why are fusible plugs installed on some aircraft wheels?
Landing Gear.a: To release tire pressure from heat buildup before a blowout.

Landing Gear.q: What are the three main types of brake actuating systems?
Landing Gear.a: Independent system, booster system, and power brake system.

Landing Gear.q: Why do large aircraft with hydraulic steering hold cylinder pressure?
Landing Gear.a: To provide steering damping.

Landing Gear.q: What is the general procedure for brake burn-in?
Landing Gear.a: Taxi at a specified speed, apply brakes smoothly, cool, and repeat.

Landing Gear.q: What safety step must be taken before wheel removal/disassembly?
Landing Gear.a: Verify the tire is completely deflated.

Landing Gear.q: What are visible signs of excessive wheel bearing heating?
Landing Gear.a: Bluish or purple discoloration of bearing surfaces.

Landing Gear.q: What precaution must be taken before accumulator disassembly?
Landing Gear.a: Discharge high-pressure gas.

Landing Gear.q: How are nose wheel steering commands transmitted on large aircraft?
Landing Gear.a: Mechanically, electrically, or hydraulically.

Landing Gear.q: When replacing Goodyear single disc brake linings, what must be done first?
Landing Gear.a: Jack and support the aircraft.

Landing Gear.q: How is nose wheel steering actuated on small aircraft?
Landing Gear.a: By mechanical linkage connected to rudder pedals.

Landing Gear.q: What prevents nose wheel vibration/oscillation?
Landing Gear.a: A shimmy dampener.

Landing Gear.q: What system maintains directional control during max braking?
Landing Gear.a: Anti-skid system.

Landing Gear.q: What is the advantage of a non-hydraulic shimmy damper?
Landing Gear.a: Long service life with no fluid servicing required.

Landing Gear.q: After repairing a wheel brake hydraulic leak, what must be done?
Landing Gear.a: Repressurize and functionally test the system.

Landing Gear.q: What type of switch is used for gear position on high-performance aircraft?
Landing Gear.a: Proximity sensors.

Landing Gear.q: What component in the anti-skid system allows testing and monitors failures?
Landing Gear.a: Anti-skid control unit.

Landing Gear.q: What is the purpose of the squat switch for landing gear?
Landing Gear.a: Prevents retraction when aircraft is on the ground.

Landing Gear.q: What are the most common power sources for landing gear extension/retraction?
Landing Gear.a: Hydraulic pressure and electric motors.

Landing Gear.q: When should a gear retraction check be performed?
Landing Gear.a: Annual/100-hour inspections, after hydraulic component replacement, or after a hard landing.

Landing Gear.q: How are bungee cords positioned on landing gear?
Landing Gear.a: Between rigid airframe structure and flexing gear assembly.

Landing Gear.q: Landing gear green down indicator lights wired in series or parallel?
Landing Gear.a: Parallel.

Landing Gear.q: What mechanisms keep nose wheel straight as it retracts?
Landing Gear.a: Internal centering cams or external tracks.

Landing Gear.q: Name several brake actuating systems.
Landing Gear.a: Independent master cylinders, boosted brakes, power-controlled brakes.

Landing Gear.q: What is the purpose of power brake de-booster cylinders?
Landing Gear.a: Reduce brake pressure and increase fluid volume flow.

Landing Gear.q: What is the function of an anti-skid system?
Landing Gear.a: Allows maximum braking effectiveness without wheel skid/lock.

Landing Gear.q: What happens if the squat switch is stuck in “weight-off” position?
Landing Gear.a: Landing gear can retract on the ground.

Landing Gear.q: Safety consideration when releasing air from strut valve core?
Landing Gear.a: Stand to the side in case the valve core ejects.

Landing Gear.q: What two conditions cause landing gear warning horn to sound?
Landing Gear.a: Gear handle up + throttle retarded/closed.

Landing Gear.q: What is the purpose of a de-booster?
Landing Gear.a: Reduces hydraulic system high pressure to lower brake pressure.

Landing Gear.q: What conditions illuminate the gear unsafe light?
Landing Gear.a: Gear handle down, squat switch weight-off, and any gear down limit not in “down.”

Landing Gear.q: What must be done before inspecting brake system for leaks?
Landing Gear.a: Pressurize the system.

Landing Gear.q: What are two methods of bleeding brakes?
Landing Gear.a: Pressure method and gravity method.

Landing Gear.q: What maintenance function most affects tire service life?
Landing Gear.a: Keeping tires properly inflated.

Landing Gear.q: Aircraft with floats require frequent inspections to detect what?
Landing Gear.a: Corrosion on metal parts.

Landing Gear.q: Where are procedures for adjusting landing gear switches found?
Landing Gear.a: Aircraft manufacturer’s maintenance manual.

Landing Gear.q: What is the most common cockpit indication gear is down and locked?
Landing Gear.a: Illuminated green light.

Landing Gear.q: Before brake system inspection for leaks, what must be done?
Landing Gear.a: Apply pressure to the system.

Landing Gear.q: What effects does tire underinflation cause?
Landing Gear.a: Wheel slippage, heat damage, premature failure, and tread wear on shoulders.

Metallic Structures.q: What is the major type of damage to aluminum structures that is caused by exposure to the weather?
Metallic Structures.a: Corrosion.

Metallic Structures.q: What are the methods used in forming sheet metal?
Metallic Structures.a: Bending, stretching, shrinking, bumping, and crimping.

Metallic Structures.q: The term smoking rivets used during aircraft inspection is an indication of what type of corrosion?
Metallic Structures.a: Fretting corrosion (commonly called smoking rivets).

Metallic Structures.q: Eddy current and ultrasonic testing are used to find what type of damage to an aircraft structure?
Metallic Structures.a: Hidden damage.

Metallic Structures.q: Although a dent may be evident, what other damage may not be obvious?
Metallic Structures.a: Distortion or failure of the substructure (hidden damage).

Metallic Structures.q: If eight rivets are used in a single-row circular patch plate, how many degrees apart should the rivets be?
Metallic Structures.a: 45 degrees (360 ÷ 8).

Metallic Structures.q: What is the size of a torch tip opening determined by?
Metallic Structures.a: It determines the amount of heat produced, not the temperature.

Metallic Structures.q: How should welding gas cylinders be transported and stored?
Metallic Structures.a: In the upright position.

Metallic Structures.q: When removing a rivet, how deep should the rivet be drilled?
Metallic Structures.a: Drill to the depth of its head.

Metallic Structures.q: Shrinking, stretching, bumping, crimping, and folding or bending are methods used for what?
Metallic Structures.a: Forming sheet metal.

Metallic Structures.q: When removing a rivet, which end should be drilled?
Metallic Structures.a: The manufactured head.

Metallic Structures.q: When TIG welding aluminum, why is flux not needed?
Metallic Structures.a: Shielding gas prevents oxide formation.

Metallic Structures.q: Shielded metal arc welding uses an inert gas to shield the molten metal from what?
Metallic Structures.a: Oxygen (to prevent oxidation).

Metallic Structures.q: What type of repair could be made for a dented steel tube cluster joint?
Metallic Structures.a: Weld a formed steel patch plate over the dented area and surrounding tubes.

Metallic Structures.q: When forming sheet metal, the radius of the bend and the thickness of the material are factors used to determine what?
Metallic Structures.a: Setback.

Metallic Structures.q: What are the dimensions of a properly formed rivet head?
Metallic Structures.a: One and one-half times the shank diameter wide and one-half the shank diameter high.

Metallic Structures.q: Describe the markings found on the heads of A, B, D, AD, and DD rivets.
Metallic Structures.a: A = plain, B = cross, D = raised dot, AD = dimple, DD = double dash.

Metallic Structures.q: What happens to the stem of a self-plugging mechanical lock rivet?
Metallic Structures.a: It is pulled until it snaps off and then trimmed flush with the head.

Metallic Structures.q: What are the different types of self-plugging mechanical lock rivets?
Metallic Structures.a: Cherrymax, Cherrylock, Olympic lock, Huck lock.

Metallic Structures.q: What does tack welding provide?
Metallic Structures.a: Controls expansion of a joint during welding.

Metallic Structures.q: What is the function of heat treatment after welding?
Metallic Structures.a: Relieves stress and normalizes the structure.

Metallic Structures.q: Name safety precautions when using a rivet gun.
Metallic Structures.a: Never point it at anyone, never depress trigger unless set is held tightly, disconnect air hose when not in use, wear safety glasses and hearing protection.

Metallic Structures.q: What type of airframe structure uses stringers made from single-piece aluminum alloy or formed aluminum?
Metallic Structures.a: Semi-monocoque type airframe.

Metallic Structures.q: What is the single-row minimum rivet spacing for protruding head rivets?
Metallic Structures.a: Three times the rivet diameter.

Metallic Structures.q: What is the difference between the tools required to pull a Cherrylock rivet and a Cherrymax rivet?
Metallic Structures.a: Cherrylock requires a tool for each size and head shape; Cherrymax can be pulled with one tool.

Metallic Structures.q: Which of the five stresses is the most common cause of rivet failure?
Metallic Structures.a: Shear stress.

Metallic Structures.q: If a 2024 rivet must be replaced with a 2117 rivet, how do you determine the size to be used?
Metallic Structures.a: For 5/32 or smaller, use the next larger size 2117 rivet (if edge distance and spacing meet minimum requirements).

Metallic Structures.q: Describe the process for determining the total length of a solid rivet for installation.
Metallic Structures.a: Add grip length (total material thickness) + 1.5 times rivet diameter.

Metallic Structures.q: What minimum edge distance and spacing should be used for a single row of protruding head rivets?
Metallic Structures.a: Not less than 2 rivet diameters from the edge and 3 diameters apart.

Metallic Structures.q: What determines the depth of a joggle?
Metallic Structures.a: Thickness of the material to be cleared.

Metallic Structures.q: Define the term bend tangent line.
Metallic Structures.a: The line where metal starts and stops bending.

Metallic Structures.q: What is the purpose of a joggle?
Metallic Structures.a: Maintains a smooth surface at a joint or splice.

Metallic Structures.q: What is the head marking for a 2117T aluminum alloy rivet?
Metallic Structures.a: A dimple.

Metallic Structures.q: What alloy is used for the rivet designated as an AD rivet?
How can a mechanic determine whether the countersink for a flush rivet should be dimpled or drilled?
Metallic Structures.a: By sheet thickness (thin = dimple, thick = countersink).

Metallic Structures.q: Define the term mold line.
Metallic Structures.a: Extension of the flat side of a part beyond the radius.

Metallic Structures.q: What action prevents cracks from forming when dimpling magnesium or hard sheet metal?
Metallic Structures.a: Use hot dimpling equipment to soften the metal.

Metallic Structures.q: What type of damage can occur if a rivet set does not properly fit?
Metallic Structures.a: Rivet head or sheet metal may be damaged.

Metallic Structures.q: How is K-Factor calculated?
Metallic Structures.a: From the K-chart (one of 179 numbers based on bend angle).

Metallic Structures.q: What is used to determine setback of a bend more or less than 90°?
Metallic Structures.a: K-Factor.

Metallic Structures.q: When extensive repairs to a fuselage are made, what may happen if a fixture is not used?
Metallic Structures.a: Parts may move or lose shape due to uneven loads.

Metallic Structures.q: Why is it important to use the proper size and weight bucking bar?
Metallic Structures.a: Too heavy = hard to control/damage; too light = won’t set rivet before work hardens.

Metallic Structures.q: If a weaker alloy must be used for a repair, what must be done?
Metallic Structures.a: Use heavier gauge metal.

Metallic Structures.q: What is the proper procedure for removing a solid shank rivet?
Metallic Structures.a: Center punch, drill to head base, drive shank out while supporting metal.

Metallic Structures.q: What are the two special calculations made when bending sheet metal?
Metallic Structures.a: Bend allowance and setback.

Metallic Structures.q: What factors determine setback?
Metallic Structures.a: Metal thickness and bend radius.

Metallic Structures.q: What is done to a corner where two bends intersect to prevent cracking?
Metallic Structures.a: Drill relief holes.

Metallic Structures.q: What are the two reasons for installing lightning holes in a wing rib?
Metallic Structures.a: Reduce weight and add strength.

Metallic Structures.q: Describe a joggle and its function.
Metallic Structures.a: Offset at an intersection to allow sheets to stack flat.

Metallic Structures.q: When repairing an all-metal aircraft, how do you determine what metals to use?
Metallic Structures.a: Use same type and thickness as original.

Metallic Structures.q: What are the three general types of welding?
Metallic Structures.a: Gas welding, electric arc, electric resistance.

Metallic Structures.q: What are some advantages of gas-shielded arc welding?
Metallic Structures.a: Stronger, more ductile, more corrosion resistant.

Metallic Structures.q: Which method of welding is less likely to cause buckling/warping of thin metal?
Metallic Structures.a: TIG welding.

Metallic Structures.q: What is the purpose of shielding an arc weld with inert gas?
Metallic Structures.a: Prevents oxygen/nitrogen contamination.

Metallic Structures.q: What is a common method of controlling expansion when welding a joint?
Metallic Structures.a: Tack welds along the joint.

Metallic Structures.q: What is the effect of inadequate penetration on a weld?
Metallic Structures.a: The weld is weak.

Metallic Structures.q: What is the purpose of flux in brazing or silver soldering?
Metallic Structures.a: Cleans the base metal by removing oxide film.

Metallic Structures.q: What type of flame is used when torch brazing or silver soldering?
Metallic Structures.a: Neutral flame.

Metallic Structures.q: What must be done to a soldering iron tip to increase heat transfer?
Metallic Structures.a: Clean and properly tin the tip.

Metallic Structures.q: What is the preferred method for welding magnesium?
Metallic Structures.a: TIG welding.

Metallic Structures.q: What is the preferred method for welding aluminum?
Metallic Structures.a: TIG welding.

Metallic Structures.q: What precautions must be observed in order to weld titanium?
Metallic Structures.a: All traces of oxygen and nitrogen must be kept away from the welded area.

Metallic Structures.q: Describe the type of repair that could be made to the Dennett steel tube cluster joint.
Metallic Structures.a: A formed steel patch plate could be welded over the damaged area.

Metallic Structures.q: How can a soft flame be obtained without reducing the heat output of the torch?
Metallic Structures.a: Use a larger tip and adjust the gas pressure accordingly.

Metallic Structures.q: What factors determine the amount of heat delivered by a gas welding torch?
Metallic Structures.a: The size of the torch tip and the gas pressure.

Metallic Structures.q: When an oxyacetylene torch is extinguished, which valve is turned off first?
Metallic Structures.a: Turn off the acetylene gas valve first.

Metallic Structures.q: When a gas welding project is completed, what safety precautions should be accomplished?
Metallic Structures.a: All the valves are turned off and the gas pressures are relieved.

Metallic Structures.q: What should be done with heat-treated aluminum parts after the completion of a welding repair?
Metallic Structures.a: The part must be reheat-treated.

Metallic Structures.q: Welding magnesium can create a serious safety hazard. What is the nature of the hazard?
Metallic Structures.a: Magnesium burns with a very hot flame that is very hard to extinguish.

Metallic Structures.q: What technique might be used to enable a tight-fitting inner sleeve to be inserted into a tubular repair?
Metallic Structures.a: The inner tube could be chilled with dry ice or in a freezer, while the outer tube could be heated slightly with a torch.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Aircraft covering fabrics are made of materials from what two sources?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Organic materials and synthetic materials.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: How would you determine the strength requirements for fabric used to recover an aircraft?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: The fabric must be at least the quality and strength of the fabric used by the original manufacturer.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What two types of organic fibers are used for aircraft covering?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Cotton and linen.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What is meant by fabric warp?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Fabric warp is the direction along the length of the fabric.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What is meant by fabric weft?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Fabric weft is the direction across the fabric at right angles to the warp.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What is meant by fabric bias?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Fabric bias is a direction at a 45-degree angle to the warp and weft.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What identification marking is found on the selvage edge and in which direction is the warp applied?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: The FAA PMA stamp is found on the selvage edge and the warp is applied parallel to the line of flight.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: How are the fabric type and strength requirements determined for fabric covered aircraft?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: They are determined by the original manufacturer and are a function of the never exceed speed and wing loading.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What types of synthetic fibers are used for covering aircraft?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Fiberglass and heat shrinkable polyester fibers such as nylon, Orion, and Dacron.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What is the purpose of reinforcing tape?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: It is used between the fabric and the rib stitching to prevent the lacing cord from cutting through the fabric.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Which type of fabric is unaffected by moisture and mildew?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Fiberglass.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Where would you use surface tape?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Over rib stitching, sewed seams, overlapped edges, around corners, and along leading and trailing edges.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: How should the edges of drainage, inspection, and ventilation holes be reinforced?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: With plastic, aluminum, or brass grommets.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What are the principal methods used to test the condition of the fabric?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Punch testers (Maul or Sable) and laboratory pull testing.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Fabric is considered unairworthy when it has deteriorated to what percentage of its original strength?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Less than 70%.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: How should a structure that will be covered with doped fabric be prepared following inspection and prior to covering?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Treat it with a protective coating of paint or varnish, as appropriate.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What is the principal advantage of the envelope method for covering wings?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Almost all seams are machine-sewn by the supplier, saving time.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What is a tie-off knot?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: A standard modified sewn knot used on all stitches except the starting stitch.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Name the three types of machine-sewn seams.
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Plain overlap, folded fell, and French fell.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Where should anti-tear strips be used?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Under wing rib stitching when the never exceed speed is greater than 250 mph.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: How would you determine the correct spacing for rib stitching if the original spacing was unknown?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Consult the chart in AC 43.13-1B.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Why is butyrate dope safer to use than nitrate dope?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: It is much less flammable.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What are the two types of dopes used for fabric finishes?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Cellulose nitrate and cellulose acetate butyrate.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What are the three forms of wood commonly used in aircraft construction?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Solid, laminated, and plywood.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What type of wood should be used when splicing or reinforcing plywood webs?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: The same type of plywood originally used.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Name the different types of defects found in wood.
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Knots, checks, splits, pitch pockets, cross grain, curly grain, decay, and dry rot.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Can a section of wood containing a hard knot be used?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Yes, within limits.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What type of glue may have been used in older wooden aircraft construction that requires careful inspection?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Casein glue.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What are the three types of glues used in modern aircraft construction and repair?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Resorcinol glue, phenol formaldehyde glue, and epoxy resin glue.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Is compression wood acceptable for structural repairs?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: No.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Why should wood pieces being joined be kept in the same room for at least 24 hours before joining?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: To allow moisture content to equalize, minimizing dimensional changes.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Why is it important to consider open assembly time when gluing structures?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: If exceeded, the glue may set up before the joint is assembled, leading to failure.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Why is it important to apply the proper clamping pressure to a glue joint?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Clamping forces out air, brings wood surfaces together evenly, and ensures strength of the glue line.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Describe methods used to apply pressure to glue joints.
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Clamps, nailing strips, power presses, straps, vacuum bags, brads, nails, and screws.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What minimum curing temperature should be observed when joining wood with adhesives?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: 70°F, or as specified by the manufacturer.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Why might stains and discoloration in wood be important during inspection?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: They usually accompany decay or rot.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Describe acceptable methods to repair elongated bolt holes in a wooden wing spar.
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Remove the section with elongated holes, splice in a new section, or replace the spar.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What species of wood is the standard for strength properties?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Sitka spruce.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: How is aircraft plywood constructed?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Made of an odd number of thin plies with grain of each at 90° to the adjacent plies.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What is laminated wood?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Two or more layers glued together with grain approximately parallel.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Difference between plywood and laminated wood?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Plywood layers are 90° to adjacent layers; laminated wood layers are parallel.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What type of joint is used to splice a solid or rectangular wood spar?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: A scarf joint.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Where are spar splices prohibited?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Under attachment fittings for wing root, landing gear, engine mounts, lift or interplane struts.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Maximum number of splices allowed in a single spar?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Two.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Describe characteristics of a scarf joint.
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Pieces tapered/beveled on slope of 1:10 or 1:12.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Why must beveled cuts be accurate on scarf joints?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Pieces must match exactly to ensure a tight glued joint.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Two primary uses of plywood in aircraft construction?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Gusset (reinforcing) plates and aircraft skin.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Types of repairs for plywood skin?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Splayed patches, surface patches, plug patches, and scarf patches.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What type of patch is used for small holes in thin plywood skin (<0.1")?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: A splayed patch, if the hole can be cleaned to less than 15 times thickness.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: How to prevent plywood patch and pressure plate from sticking if glue extrudes?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Place wax paper or vinyl plastic between them.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Why are lightweight steel bushings used in wooden structures?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Prevent wood crushing when bolts are tightened.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Purpose of large surface area washers on wooden structures?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Provide extra bearing area and prevent damage.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: How do you inspect wood structures for rot?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Visual inspection with a light.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: When manufacturer’s manuals are not available, what FAA advisory circular is used?
What is the common term for advanced wood damage that crushes into powder?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Dry rot.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What causes dark discoloration or grey stains along wood grain?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Water penetration.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: When are mineral streaks acceptable in wood structures?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: When no decay is found.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Glue joints are designed to take what type of loads?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Shear loads.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What happens if bolt holes in wood are too tight and forced with heavy blows?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: May cause splitting or unequal load distribution.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: In the case of elongated bolt holes in a spar or cracks in close proximity to the bolt holes, what may be required of the repair?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: The repair may require a new section to be spliced in the spar or replacement of the entire spar.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What are the two factors that allow a doped-on patch repair?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: The size of the opening and the aircraft VNE speed.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What must be done prior to repairing a tear in the fabric by sewing the edges together?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Remove the old dope coats down to the clear dope coats, a distance of two inches on each side of the opening.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Why is compression wood not acceptable in aircraft structures?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: It does not have the required strength for aircraft structures.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What are the three ways to gain FAA approval to recover an aircraft with materials and processes other than those with which it was originally certificated?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: By supplemental type certificate, FAA Form 337, or through a change to the TCDS.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What is a fabric selvage edge?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: An edge of cloth woven to prevent raveling.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What knots are used between the first and last knots when rib lacing?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Modified seine knots.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What can be caused by repeated pulling of long lengths of lacing cord?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: It may remove the wax coating from the cord and cause fraying.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: A DC-3 Aileron is being recovered, but the rib lace spacing was not recorded prior to disposing of the old fabric. How can the correct rib lace spacing be determined?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Refer to the DC-3 maintenance manual or the general rib lace spacing table in AC-43.13-1B.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What is a likely result to the protective fabric covering if a split should occur in the underlying plywood skin?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: The split in the plywood skin will initiate a similar defect in the fabric covering.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What is the maximum allowable deterioration for aircraft fabric strength?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: 30% of the original fabric strength.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: How may the core of a composite material be further damaged if a proper inspection is not performed?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Liquid ingression into the core may occur.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: When a drill bit is being used for drilling composites, it should be operated at approximately what speed?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Use an air-driven tool capable of a free speed of up to 20,000 rpm.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What are the most common causes for delamination of a composite structure?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Sonic vibration, expansion of internal moisture, liquid leakage, and manufacturing defects (air pockets).

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Name the factors that cause crazing in transparent plastic windows and windshields.
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Exposure to ultraviolet light, stress, solvents, and improper handling.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: How should a hole be drilled in plexiglass to avoid damage to the hole when the drill breaks through the underside?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Back up the plastic with a piece of wood and feed the drill slowly.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What should be done to twist drills that are to be used for drilling completely through plexiglass?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Modify to a 60° tip angle, zero rake angle, and increase the back tip clearance angle to 12–15°.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What is a material called that consists of two relatively thin, parallel face sheets bonded to and separated by a relatively thick, lightweight core?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Bonded honeycomb composite sandwich construction.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Name the different core materials used in bonded honeycomb construction.
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Kraft paper, thermoplastics, aluminum, steel, titanium, aramid paper, fiberglass, carbon, and ceramics.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Who can extend the shelf life of properly stored, uncured prepreg?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Only the material manufacturer, after testing.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: During the repair of a damaged bonded honeycomb, what is done with the damaged area?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: The damaged area must be removed and trimmed out to the face sheet in a smooth shape with rounded corners, circular, or oval shape.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What can be used to repair damage to a sandwich honeycomb structure that is smaller than 0.5 inch?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: A potted filler repair.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What is the main difference between thermoplastic and thermosetting resin plastics?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Thermoplastics can be softened by heat and rehardened; thermosetting resins do not soften with heat and will char or burn.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: When rivets are used while installing acrylic plastics, what is used to prevent excessive tightening of the frame to the plastics?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Spacers or what the manufacturer specifies.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Why do epoxy resins require refrigeration until they are used?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: To prevent aging of the epoxy.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: How should large quantities of quick-curing resin be mixed to prevent overheating and poisonous smoke?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: Mix only the amount of material that is required.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What may result from the use of adhesives in the repair of aircraft structures that are not acceptable to the FAA?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: The adhesive may not meet all necessary performance requirements.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What may result if materials such as pre-preg, film adhesive, or foaming adhesive are accidentally stored in a freezer with an automatic defrost cycle?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: The shelf life of the material could be reduced due to warming during the defrost cycle.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: Unless specified by the aircraft manufacturer, what should screws removed from aircraft structures be replaced with?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: New screws of identical length but one gauge larger in diameter.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: What is the limiting factor of polishing and finishing an acrylic windshield?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: No sanding that could affect optical properties or distort the pilot’s vision should be done.

Nonmetallic Structures.q: When drilling acrylic plastic, what is the recommended included angle of the tip of the drill bit?
Nonmetallic Structures.a: 140 degrees.

Rotorcraft.q: What is used to counteract the torque produced by the rotation of the main rotor blades?
Rotorcraft.a: The anti-torque rotor or the tail rotor is used.

Rotorcraft.q: What compensates for helicopter main rotor torque?
Rotorcraft.a: An auxiliary or tail rotor compensates for main rotor torque.

Rotorcraft.q: What is the purpose of a rotorcraft cyclic pitch control?
Rotorcraft.a: The purpose of the cyclic pitch control is to allow the pilot to fly the helicopter in any horizontal direction.

Rotorcraft.q: What is the purpose of the rotorcraft collective pitch control?
Rotorcraft.a: It changes the pitch of the main rotor blades simultaneously.

Rotorcraft.q: Name the three types of rotor systems found on helicopters.
What is the purpose of the swash plate assembly?
Rotorcraft.a: It transmits control inputs from the collective and cyclic pitch controls to the main rotor blades.

Rotorcraft.q: Main rotor frequency vibrations fall into four types. Which frequency vibration is caused by a loose airframe component?
What is the source of high frequency vibration in a rotorcraft?
Rotorcraft.a: High frequency vibration can be caused by anything that rotates or vibrates at extremely high speeds.

Rotorcraft.q: Movement about the longitudinal axis in a helicopter is affected by the movement of what?
Rotorcraft.a: Moving the cyclic pitch control right or left.

Rotorcraft.q: Movement about the lateral axis in a helicopter is affected by the movement of what?
Rotorcraft.a: Moving the cyclic pitch control fore or aft.

Rotorcraft.q: In a hovering helicopter equipped with a tail rotor, directional control is maintained by what?
Rotorcraft.a: By varying the pitch of the tail rotor blades with the foot pedals.

Rotorcraft.q: How does a helicopter in forward flight change direction?
Rotorcraft.a: With the cyclic pitch control, tilting the main rotor disk in the desired direction.

Rotorcraft.q: What is the purpose of the freewheeling unit in a helicopter drive system?
Rotorcraft.a: It disengages the engine from the main rotor when the engine RPM is less than the rotor RPM.

Rotorcraft.q: What is the purpose of checking main rotor blade tracking on a helicopter?
Rotorcraft.a: The purpose is to bring the tips of all blades into the same tip path of rotation.

Rotorcraft.q: Main rotor blades that do not cone by the same amount during rotation are said to be out of what?
How does blade flapping help to compensate for dissymmetry of lift in helicopter main rotor systems?
Rotorcraft.a: Blade flapping serves to increase the angle of attack and lift on the retreating blade side of the rotor and decreases the angle of attack and lift on the advancing blade side.

Rotorcraft.q: How does the airflow through the main rotor of a helicopter change during the transition from normal flight to autorotation?
Rotorcraft.a: During normal flight the air moves downward, and during autorotation the air moves upward.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: Where are generator rating and performance data located?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: Generator rating and performance data are stamped on the nameplate, attached to the generator.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: Why is it important to know the alternator manufacturer prescribed output ratings?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: To ensure the total electrical load of the equipment does not exceed the output limits of the alternator.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: When comparison shows that the total connected electrical load can exceed the output limits of the alternator, what must be done?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: The load should be reduced so that an overload cannot occur.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: Why is a series wound motor commonly used as an aircraft engine starter?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: Because it is able to develop a high starting torque under a heavy load.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: What are the four major parts of a DC motor?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: The armature assembly, the field assembly, the brush assembly, and the end frame.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: What are two components of a direct cranking electric starter system?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: An electric motor, reduction gears, and an automatic engaging and disengaging mechanism.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: What DC system component prevents the battery from discharging into the generator when the battery voltage is more than the generator voltage?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: The reverse current cutout relay.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: What is used to control DC generator voltage?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: Regulation of the field current strength.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: How does a voltage regulator control the voltage of an alternator?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: By varying the strength of the AC exciter stationary fields.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: What is the maximum allowable voltage drop between the generator and the bus bar?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: 2% of the regulated voltage.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: What two things determine the frequency of alternator output?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: The speed of rotation of the rotor and the number of poles.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: When selecting a wire size, what four factors are taken into account for the selection?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: Power loss, voltage drop, current carrying ability, and conductor material.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: When selecting the proper size of wire conductor, what must be known about the operation of the circuit?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: Whether the current to be carried is intermittent or continuous.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: When adding additional electrical equipment to an aircraft, why is it important to know the maximum load requirements for the new equipment, along with the current load requirement of the aircraft system?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: To ensure the new load plus the previous maximum load do not exceed the rated limits of the existing wires, cables, or protection devices.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: When the constant speed drive and the alternator are contained in one unit, this is known as what?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: An IDG (Integrated Drive Generator).

Engine Electrical Systems.q: What component ensures the alternator rotates at the correct speed to ensure a 400 Hz frequency?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: The constant speed drive (CSD).

Engine Electrical Systems.q: What is the maximum slack allowed between the supports of a single wire or bundle installation?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: Slack should not exceed ½ inch.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: How are wires that are running close to exhaust stacks or heating ducts protected?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: They must be insulated with high temperature rated material (asbestos, teflon, or fiberglass).

Engine Electrical Systems.q: What is the size standard for electrical wire used in U.S. manufactured aircraft?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: The American Wire Gauge (AWG) standard.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: When removing the leads of a battery, which lead should be removed first?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: Always remove the grounding lead first and reinstall it last.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: Who determines the electrical load limits that can be imposed on the alternator by installed equipment?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: The manufacturer.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: When should starter motor brushes be replaced?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: When worn down to approximately one-half the original length.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: How may a glazed or dirty starter commutator be cleaned?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: By holding a strip of double-aught sandpaper or a brush-seeding stone against the commutator as it turns.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: When more than one generator is used in parallel, what is the total rated output power?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: The combined output power of the installed generators.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: What is the function of a constant speed drive in an AC generating system?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: To keep the three-phase AC generator at a constant frequency.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: Why should bonding jumpers be kept as short as practicable?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: So that the resistance of each connection does not exceed 0.003 ohm.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: How should bonding jumpers be installed?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: They should not interfere with the operation of movable aircraft elements (e.g., control surfaces).

Engine Electrical Systems.q: Why are wire bundles laced or tied with cord?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: To provide ease of installation, maintenance, and inspection.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: Explain why flat nylon braided wax lacing tape should be used for lacing or tying any wire bundles containing coaxial cables.
Engine Electrical Systems.a: Elastic materials or overtightening can deform the interconductor insulation, causing short circuits or impedance changes.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: How many phases are used in most aircraft AC alternator systems?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: Three phases.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: Why are multiple generator systems paralleled?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: To ensure each generator shares the load equally.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: What DC alternator system component prevents battery reverse current flow through the armature?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: The reverse current relay or rectifier.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: What does an ammeter or load meter indicate when it is connected to the generator output lead and the engine is operating?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: The current leaving the generator to the electrical system load.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: What does an ammeter or load meter indicate when it is connected to the battery positive lead and the engine is operating?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: Whether the generator is supplying enough current to charge the battery, or if the battery is discharging.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: What is the purpose of bonding wires on power plant installations?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: To provide current return paths, prevent static discharge, and protect the aircraft and personnel from lightning discharge.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: What is an indication of a dirty or worn starter commutator?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: Excessive noise and dragging in the starter.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: What type of DC motor is commonly used for a reciprocating engine starter?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: A series wound motor.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: Which wire size is larger: AWG10 or AWG14?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: AWG10.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: What are the operating modes of a turbine engine starter generator?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: It operates first as a starter, then as a generator after the engine is operating.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: What are the components of a direct cranking electric starter system?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: An electric motor, reduction gears, and an automatic engaging and disengaging mechanism.

Engine Electrical Systems.q: How does a voltage regulator control the voltage of an alternator?
Engine Electrical Systems.a: By regulating the voltage output of the DC exciter.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: What is a common cause of a turbocharger wastegate sticking?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: Coke deposits or carbon buildup.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: In using collector-type exhaust systems, what problem is offset when used on turbocharged engines?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: A loss of horsepower due to an increase in exhaust system back pressure.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: How are exhaust system internal baffles or diffusers inspected?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: The exhaust system is disassembled as necessary for visual inspection.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: What are two types of reciprocating engine exhaust systems?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: The short stack system and the collector system.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: What type of exhaust system is used on turbocharged engines?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: The collector system.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: Identify two severe hazards of an exhaust system failure.
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: Carbon monoxide poisoning, loss of engine power, or fire.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: What is an indication of an exhaust gas leak?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: A flat gray or sooty black deposit in the area of the leak.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: What are the three components of a turbine engine exhaust duct?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: Tail cone, exhaust duct (tailpipe), and support struts.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: What are the two things that happen to turbine engine exhaust gases when the exhaust nozzle opening is changed?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: It changes the density and velocity of the exhaust gases.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: Name two categories of thrust reverser systems.
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: Mechanical blockage (clamshell) and aerodynamic blockage (cascade).

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: What action should be done when performing any type of maintenance on the thrust reverser system?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: The system must be mechanically locked out from deploying while personnel are in the area.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: Where are exhaust manifold and stack failures usually found?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: At welded or clamped points, flange-to-stack, manifold attachments, crossover pipes, and muffler connections.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: Internal failures of the exhaust system on a reciprocating engine are usually the result of what?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: Erosion and carburization from extreme thermal conditions, or local hot spots causing burning, bulging, or rupture.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: If a component of the exhaust system is inaccessible for visual inspection, how is it best checked for leaks?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: Plug openings, apply about 2 psi internal air pressure, and submerge in water to detect bubbles.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: During inspection, the outer muffler wall is found bulging or ruptured. What is the likely cause?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: A local hot spot caused by uneven exhaust gas flow.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: What is the main function of the exhaust system on a reciprocating engine?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: To dispose of gases safely for the airframe and occupants.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: When is the collector exhaust system used on an aircraft with reciprocating engines?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: On most large non-supercharged engines, all turbo-supercharged engines, and installations where it improves streamlining or maintenance access.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: What material is typically used to make exhaust system muffler shrouds?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: Stainless steel.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: What drawback in using collector-type exhaust systems is more than offset when used on turbocharged engines?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: The loss of horsepower due to exhaust system back pressure.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: Why should lead, zinc, or galvanized marks not be made on exhaust systems?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: They cause a molecular structure change when heated, leading to cracks.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: How can failure of exhaust system internal baffles or diffusers cause a loss of engine power?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: By restricting the flow of exhaust gases.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: What happens to engine power when a heat exchanger leaks exhaust gases into the induction system?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: There will be a loss of power.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: What are exhaust system coke deposits?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: Excessive carbon buildup.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: Why are turbocharged exhaust system leaks very damaging at high altitudes?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: The pressure differential makes the leak escape with torch-like intensity.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: What are the hazards of operating thrust reversers at low ground speeds?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: Re-ingestion of hot exhaust gases and ingestion of foreign objects.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: Are thrust reversers generally able to produce maximum reverse thrust equal to full forward thrust?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: No, reverse thrust is substantially less than forward thrust.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: What change does the noise suppressor make to noise frequency to reduce older turbojet engine noise?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: It converts low-frequency sound into high-frequency sound.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: What is the function of thermocouple probes in a turbine exhaust pipe?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: To measure exhaust gas temperature.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: What are thrust reversers primarily used for?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: To aid in decelerating an aircraft after landing.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.q: Why is exhaust system failure considered a severe hazard?
Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.a: It can cause carbon monoxide poisoning, loss of engine power, or fire.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: What is an area or region of an aircraft designated by the manufacturer to require fire detection and/or fire extinguishing equipment?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: It is called the fire zone.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: What happens to a thermal switch fire protection system when it is heated enough to operate?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: The thermal switch closes, completing the light circuit, which indicates a fire or overheat condition.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: How is a Fenwal continuous loop fire detection system constructed?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: The Fenwal system uses a slender Inconel tube packed with thermally sensitive eutectic salt and a nickel wire center conductor.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: What is a type of fire detection system that permits more complete coverage of an area than the spot type temperature detectors?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: It is the continuous loop system.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: Name the two types of fire detection systems installed in aircraft to detect engine fires.
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: The two types of systems are spot detector and continuous loop.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: What is likely to happen when an engine fire sensing loop is excessively kinked or bent?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: It will likely cause a false fire warning.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: How is the fire extinguishing agent in a high rate of discharge system distributed?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: It is distributed by using open-end tubes.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: What are two delivery components used to distribute fire extinguishing agents into a fire?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: They are perforated tubing and discharge nozzles.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: What do missing colored discs in a fire extinguisher system indicate?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: The discs indicate a discharge.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: What is the proper method for determining the fire extinguisher container pressure?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: Use the container pressure gauge with the pressure-temperature curve chart.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: What are two ways that fire extinguishing agents are distributed to contain the fire in the powerplant zones?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: By using perforated tubing or discharge nozzles.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: Continuous loop systems are versions of what type of fire detecting system?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: Thermal switch system.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: When the fire has been extinguished or the critical temperature lowered, the fenwal system automatically does what?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: It automatically returns to standby alert, ready to detect any subsequent fire or overheat condition.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: What does a short do to the fire detection system?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: A short disables the fire detection system.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: What must happen in a dual-loop system to receive a fire warning?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: Both loops must signal to result in a fire warning.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: How do optical sensors detect fires?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: By detecting prominent specific radiation emissions from hydrocarbon flames.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: What does a complete fire protection system include?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: Both a fire detection and a fire extinguishing system.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: What happens when the fire switch is activated?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: The engine stops (fuel control shuts off), the engine is isolated from aircraft systems, and the fire extinguishing system is activated.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: When are the red and yellow disks associated with a fire extinguishing system visible?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: Both disks are visible when the system has not been discharged.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: What are the essential ingredients necessary for any fire to occur?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: Ignition source, oxygen, and fuel.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: What is the purpose of a discharge cartridge and how is it activated?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: The electrically operated discharge cartridge releases the fire extinguishing agent.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: What is a HRD fire extinguisher system?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: A system with a high rate of discharge.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: What indicates low agent pressure in a container in a fire extinguisher system?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: A pressure gauge.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: What are the red and yellow disks associated with a fire extinguisher system used to indicate?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: Red = thermal discharge; Yellow = normal discharge.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: What group of fire extinguishing agents are no longer being manufactured due to environmental concerns?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: Halons, Freons, or Chlorofluorocarbons.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: How long does it take to discharge a HRD system?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: 1–2 seconds.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: What is the purpose of a fire extinguishing system?
