Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: To dilute the atmosphere around a fire with an inert agent that will not support combustion.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: What are the different types of fire detector systems used for engine fire protection?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: Overheat, Rate of Rise, and Flame Detectors.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: What will happen in a thermocouple fire warning system on an engine that overheats slowly?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: Nothing, because it takes a fast rate of rise (rapid heating) to operate.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: What is the principle of operation of a KIDDE continuous loop fire protection system?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: Two wires are embedded in a ceramic core inside an Inconel tube. One wire is ground, the other is hot; the ceramic’s resistance changes with temperature, providing current.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: What is the minimum number of thermal switch units needed for a thermal switch fire protection system?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: At least one.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.q: What electrical power is needed for a thermocouple fire protection system?
Engine Fire Protection Systems.a: Aircraft electrical system and thermocouple-produced voltage.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What operates an automatic mixture control?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: It operates by a sealed bellows that contracts or expands with a change in pressure and temperature.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What are the two types of carburetors most commonly used on small reciprocating engines?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Float-type carburetors and pressure-type carburetors.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What is another type of fuel metering system used on reciprocating engines other than carburetors?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Direct fuel injection.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What type of fuel boost pump is commonly used?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: The centrifugal type pump.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: Name the advantages of a FADEC over a hydromechanical fuel control.
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Requires no engine trimming; improved engine starts; saves fuel; provides engine limit protection; constant idle speed with changes in conditions; fully modulates active clearance control; repeatable engine transients.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What is the RPM indication when an idle mixture is set correctly and the mixture control is placed to idle cutoff?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: A slight rise in RPM prior to decreasing.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What are the different causes of fuel vapor lock?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Low fuel pressure, high fuel temperature, and excessive fuel turbulence.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: Name two major components of a supervisory electronic engine control.
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: The electronic control unit (computer) and hydro-mechanical fuel control.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: In a reciprocating engine, what problem is caused by an excessively rich mixture at idle?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Spark plug fouling.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What is the purpose of a carburetor-economizer system?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Provides additional fuel at high power settings for cooling to prevent detonation.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What is the function of a mixture control on float-type carburetors?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Determines the fuel-to-air ratio.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: When does a carburetor economizer system needle valve begin to open?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: At 60–70% of rated power.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: Why should engine RPM be accelerated periodically when making carburetor adjustments?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: To clear excess fuel from the carburetor (“clearing the engine”).

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What are the three most common types of fuel filters used?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Micron filter, wafer screen filter, and plain screen mesh filter.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: Why is a low-pressure filter installed between the supply tanks and the engine fuel system?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: To protect the engine-driven fuel pump and various control devices.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What are the purposes of fuel selector valves?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: To provide a means for shutting fuel flow off, tank and engine selection, crossfeed, and fuel transfer.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: Particles of rubber in a mainline strainer are early warnings of what?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Hose deterioration.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: An additional high-pressure fuel filter is installed where?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Between the fuel pump and the fuel control.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What are the two most widely used fuel filters?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: 200 mesh and 35 mesh micron filters.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What unit in a turbine engine fuel system is susceptible to the formation of ice?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: The fuel filter.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What methods are most often used to prevent ice in turbine engine fuel filters?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: A heat exchanger using bleed air or engine oil.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What are the three components of the EEC system?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Hydromechanical fuel control, electronic fuel control unit, and power lever angle potentiometer.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What is the action of a float-type carburetor accelerating system when the throttle is opened quickly?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Supplies extra fuel during sudden increases in power.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: When there is an engine-driven fuel pump in the system, what should be looked for during inspection?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Leaks and security of mounting.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: Where does a pressure injection carburetor obtain fuel pressure?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: From an engine-driven fuel pump.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: On a fuel injection engine, at what point does the fuel mix with the air?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: At the inlet port to the cylinder.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What position of a pressure injection carburetor manual mixture control would stop engine operation?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Idle cutoff.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What are the three functions of the Venturi?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Proportions air-fuel mixture, decreases pressure at the discharge nozzle, and limits airflow at full throttle.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: Name the advantages of direct fuel injection over carburetor systems.
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Less danger of icing, improved acceleration, improved fuel distribution, reduced cylinder overheating, and better fuel economy.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: In turbine fuel control systems, which type is most accurate?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Electronically controlled fuel control.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: If fuel is leaking from the discharge nozzle of a carburetor when the engine is not running, what is the cause?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Leaking float needle valve and seat.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: In the FADEC system, what unit meters the fuel flow?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: The electronic engine control (EEC).

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: A turbine fuel control system without electronic interface is known as what?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Hydromechanical system.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: On a reciprocating engine, the injection carburetor that employs a closed feed system is known as what?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Hydromechanical fuel control system.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What is the safety feature when a micron filter is clogged?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Bypass valve.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What is the purpose of a turbine engine duplex fuel nozzle?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Produces two spray patterns.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What turbine component injects fuel into the combustion area?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Fuel spray nozzles.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What instrument indication is used when trimming turbine engines?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: RPM.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: If a float-type carburetor engine fails to develop full power, what is the first thing to check?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Throttle control adjustment.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: How does a closed-feed injection carburetor operate?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Fuel is metered through fixed jets according to airflow and discharged under positive pressure.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What happens if the air-fuel ratio is enriched above 0.080?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Loss of power and lower temperature.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: Name the engine variables detected by a turbine engine fuel control unit.
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Power lever position, RPM, compressor inlet temp, compressor inlet pressure, compressor discharge pressure, and burner pressure.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What are the two sources for fuel heating in turbine systems?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Bleed air and engine oil.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What is the function of a turbine engine fuel control unit?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Schedules fuel flow to match power demand.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What requirement must reciprocating fuel metering systems meet?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Meter fuel proportionately to air to maintain proper ratio at all speeds and altitudes.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What are the three basic types of turbine fuel control units?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: FADEC, hydromechanical electronic, and hydromechanical.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: Why must fuel lines have gentle slopes and no sharp curves?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: To prevent vapor lock.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What happens to excess fuel not required by a constant displacement pump?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: A relief valve bypasses excess back to the pump inlet.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: A gear type pump is classified as what?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Constant (positive) displacement.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: Why may an electric boost pump be used in an APU?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: To supply fuel under pressure to the control.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What does the use of fuel boost pumps help prevent?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Vapor lock by keeping lines pressurized.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What is the best stoichiometric air-fuel mixture?
What are the two categories of turbine engine fuel pumps?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Constant and variable displacement.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What is the purpose of the fuel pressurizing valve?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Divides flow into primary and secondary manifolds of the duplex nozzle.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: When the mixture is moved to cutoff and RPM drops immediately to zero, what does that indicate?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Idle mixture set too lean.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What problem occurs if idle mixture is set too rich?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Spark plug fouling.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What type of engine-driven fuel pump is widely used other than gear and piston pumps?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Rotary vane pump.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What is the purpose of the dump portion of a pressurizing and dump valve?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Drains fuel from manifolds after shutdown.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What is the purpose of the fuel pressurizing valve?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Provides primary and secondary fuel flow to dual-line duplex nozzles.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What keeps a dual-element constant displacement fuel pump operating if one gear seizes?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: The shear section of the driveshaft.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What type of fuel pump can vary the discharge regardless of speed?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Variable displacement pump.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: Why is vapor lock serious?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: It can completely block fuel flow.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.q: What are the purposes of the main fuel strainers?
Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.a: Collect water and sediment, keep foreign matter out of carburetor.

Engine Inspection.q: What may be used as a guide for 100-hour inspections on aircraft engines?
Engine Inspection.a: The minimum scope and detail of items in FAR Part 43, Appendix D, and if available, the manufacturer’s maintenance manual checklist.

Engine Inspection.q: What additional inspection must be performed if a cylinder compression is weak?
Engine Inspection.a: An internal cylinder inspection must be performed.

Engine Inspection.q: What items must be included in the operational check of a reciprocating engine prior to approval for return to service after a 100-hour inspection?
Engine Inspection.a: Power output, static and idle RPM, magneto check, fuel pressure, oil pressure, cylinder temperature, oil temperature.

Engine Inspection.q: Where is the identifying information of an engine located?
Engine Inspection.a: On the engine data plate.

Engine Inspection.q: When may a mechanic incorporate a major alteration to an aircraft based on a supplemental type certificate?
Engine Inspection.a: Only if the owner/operator holds the STC or has written permission from the holder.

Engine Inspection.q: What publication is needed to check an engine for normal operation?
Engine Inspection.a: The manufacturer’s maintenance manual.

Engine Inspection.q: If the installed spark plug is not listed in Note 8 of the TCDS, how would you determine if the spark plug is approved for use in the engine?
Engine Inspection.a: Refer to the engine manufacturer’s service information for approved spark plugs for that engine model.

Engine Inspection.q: Name where engine operating limitations may be found.
Engine Inspection.a: Type Certificate Data Sheet, Engine Specifications, Engine Maintenance Manual, Aircraft Manufacturer’s Maintenance Manual.

Engine Inspection.q: What is indicated by dished piston heads, collapsed valve heads, broken ring lands, or eroded portions of valves, pistons, or cylinder heads after overhaul?
Engine Inspection.a: Indications of detonation.

Engine Inspection.q: What are normally used to secure reciprocating engine mounts to the aircraft?
Engine Inspection.a: Special, heat-treated bolts.

Engine Inspection.q: If a recurring AD is found to be past due for compliance, what should be done?
Engine Inspection.a: The aircraft is unairworthy until the AD is complied with; the owner must be provided a written list of discrepancies.

Engine Inspection.q: When performing maintenance on the flight line, what precautions should be taken to prevent hearing loss?
Engine Inspection.a: Always wear hearing protection.

Engine Inspection.q: Next to valve blow-by, what is the most frequent cause of compression leakage?
Engine Inspection.a: Excessive leakage past the piston rings.

Engine Inspection.q: What does the cylinder compression test determine?
Engine Inspection.a: If the valves, piston rings, and pistons are adequately sealing the combustion chamber.

Engine Inspection.q: In the TCDS for a reciprocating engine, where can the approved magnetos be found?
Engine Inspection.a: In the notes section of the TCDS.

Engine Inspection.q: If a manufacturer’s service bulletin conflicts with an airworthiness directive, which takes precedence?
Engine Inspection.a: The airworthiness directive.

Engine Inspection.q: When are manufacturer’s service bulletins mandatory?
Engine Inspection.a: When referenced in an AD or required in an approved inspection program.

Engine Inspection.q: What are the three main components of a spark plug?
Engine Inspection.a: Electrode, insulator, and outer shell.

Engine Inspection.q: What are the three primary causes of pre-ignition?
Engine Inspection.a: Carbon/hot deposits, detonation, and excessively lean mixture at high power.

Engine Inspection.q: What fuel-air mixture results in after-firing?
Engine Inspection.a: A mixture that is too rich.

Engine Inspection.q: Where in the engine control movement should cushion action be felt?
Engine Inspection.a: At full open and full closed positions.

Engine Inspection.q: What is the purpose of engine control cushion?
Engine Inspection.a: Ensures throttle valves open fully and close to proper idle speed, not limited by quadrant stops.

Engine Inspection.q: Most engine mounts for turbofan engines are made of what?
Engine Inspection.a: Stainless steel.

Engine Inspection.q: What publication is needed to inspect an engine for conformity with specifications?
Engine Inspection.a: Type Certificate Data Sheet (engine specifications).

Engine Inspection.q: How do you identify the engine serial number?
Engine Inspection.a: On the engine data plate.

Engine Inspection.q: Where are life-limited parts of an engine listed?
Engine Inspection.a: Airworthiness Limitations section of ICA, Engine Maintenance Manual, Engine TCDS.

Engine Inspection.q: What criteria are used to determine the replacement of life-limited turbine engine components?
Engine Inspection.a: Number of cycles, hours, or calendar time.

Engine Inspection.q: Why are hot section inspections performed on turbine engines?
Engine Inspection.a: To determine the integrity and wear of hot section components.

Engine Inspection.q: What inspections must be performed if EGT exceeds limitations?
Engine Inspection.a: A hot section inspection.

Engine Inspection.q: What inspections must be performed following a turbine engine overspeed?
Engine Inspection.a: Refer to manufacturer’s maintenance manual.

Engine Inspection.q: Name two inspection requirements when a reciprocating engine is removed from storage.
Engine Inspection.a: Inspect for corrosion, remove/clean oil screens, remove desiccant bags, check for excess preservative.

Engine Inspection.q: What FAA approvals are required when installing an engine not on the aircraft TCDS?
Engine Inspection.a: A supplemental type certificate or an FAA field approval.

Engine Inspection.q: What FAA approvals are required when an engine design change doesn’t require a new type certificate?
Engine Inspection.a: A supplemental type certificate or FAA field approval under minor type design change.

Engine Inspection.q: What purpose does oil analysis serve?
Engine Inspection.a: Evaluates engine health; metal particles show normal or abnormal wear.

Engine Inspection.q: Under what conditions is compliance with a service bulletin mandatory?
Engine Inspection.a: When an AD references it, when required by approved operating specs, or at overhaul if specified.

Engine Inspection.q: Why must turbojet/turbofan turbine blades be reinstalled in the same position?
Engine Inspection.a: To maintain balance of the turbine wheel.

Engine Inspection.q: What is fan blade shingling?
Engine Inspection.a: Overlapping of mid-span shrouds of fan blades.

Engine Inspection.q: What publication is used to check an engine for normal operation?
Engine Inspection.a: Manufacturer’s maintenance manual.

Engine Inspection.q: What inspection should be accomplished during coast down after shutdown of a turbofan/turbojet engine?
Engine Inspection.a: Listen for rubbing sounds or unusual noises.

Engine Inspection.q: What functions must be done to prepare an engine for a 100-hour inspection?
Engine Inspection.a: Remove cowling and thoroughly clean the engine.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: What is the unit of measure for a reciprocating engine fuel flow system?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: Gallons per hour.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: What is the unit of measure for a turbine engine fuel flow system?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: Pounds per hour.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: What are the two main components of a turbine engine fuel flow indicating system?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: A transmitter and an indicator.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: What are the two reasons for monitoring fuel flow?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: Indication of fuel consumption and engine performance.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: What does turbine engine exhaust gas temperature (EGT) indicate?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: The average temperature measured by individual thermocouples.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: Where does the carburetor air temperature indicator on a reciprocating engine receive its signal?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: From a bulb or electric sensor.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: What would be the result of resistance changes in a turbine exhaust gas temperature circuit?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: Erroneous temperature readings.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: What is the source of electrical power for a cylinder head temperature system?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: A thermocouple.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: Where is the carburetor air temperature bulb or sensor located?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: In the RAM air intake duct at the carburetor entrance.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: Where should the thermocouple be installed on a single probe cylinder head temperature system?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: On the hottest cylinder.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: What does a tachometer for a reciprocating engine indicate?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: Engine crankshaft RPM.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: What is the purpose of a tachometer on a turbine engine?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: Indicates percentage of the compressor section speed.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: When checking the manifold pressure of a reciprocating engine with a controllable propeller, what position should the blades be set?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: Against their low pitch stops.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: What directly controls the manifold pressure of a reciprocating engine?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: Engine RPM and throttle opening.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: On a reciprocating engine with a fixed-pitch propeller, what controls both manifold pressure and engine RPM?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: The throttle control.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: What does a manifold pressure gauge on a reciprocating engine indicate?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: Absolute pressure in inches of mercury.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: What does an illuminated red enunciator light normally indicate?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: A dangerous condition.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: What does turbine engine pressure ratio indicate?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: Engine thrust or power produced.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: What pressures are measured to obtain turbine engine pressure ratio?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: Total inlet pressure (PT2) and total exhaust pressure (PT7).

Engine Instrument Systems.q: On a fire extinguishing system for a large aircraft, what do the indicating lights and crew alerting system messages notify the crew of?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: When fire bottle pressure is low.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: What two range markings are found on an oil temperature gauge of a reciprocating aircraft engine?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: A green arc and a red radial line.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: When installing cylinder head temperature leads and they are excessively long, what should you do?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: Coil up the leads and tie off the excess.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: Where is the thermocouple for the cylinder head temperature gauge located?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: In a special gasket under a rear spark plug, or in a special well in the top or rear of the cylinder head.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: What would occur if the thermocouple lead is cut to shorten it?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: Incorrect temperature readings.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: Which cylinder is the cylinder head temperature probe attached to?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: The hottest cylinder in that engine installation.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: What is the limitation of a single-cylinder cylinder head temperature installation?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: It only gives evidence of general engine temperature.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: Why do turbine engines use percent RPM indicators?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: Because of the very high RPM they operate at.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: In what units is a reciprocating engine tachometer calibrated?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: Hundreds, with graduations at every 100 RPM.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: What does the red radial line indicate on a tachometer?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: Maximum permissible RPM.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: Where is the carburetor temperature bulb located?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: In the ram air intake duct.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: What is the fuel pressure range on a float-type carburetor?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: 3 to 5 psi.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: What unit of measurement is generally used to indicate fuel flow?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: Pounds or gallons per hour.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: What does the manifold pressure gauge indicate and how is it calibrated?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: Absolute pressure in the engine manifold, calibrated in inches of mercury.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: During engine operation, where should the propeller control be set when checking manifold pressure?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: Low pitch, high RPM.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: What are the possible indications of an oil pressure transmitter or indicator malfunction?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: A sudden or severe drop in oil pressure while oil temp remains normal and supply remains full.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: Is the aircraft electrical system required to power a cylinder head temperature gauge?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: No, it uses a thermocouple as the source.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: In a carbureted reciprocating engine, where is the fuel flow indicating system generally connected?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: Between the engine fuel pump and the carburetor.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: How is turbine engine EGT obtained?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: By several thermocouples spaced around the exhaust duct near the turbine exit.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: The torque being developed by a turboprop engine is an indication of what?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: Engine shaft horsepower.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: If oil pressure drops off severely, oil supply remains full, and temperature is normal, what is the possible cause?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: A malfunctioning oil pressure transmitter or indicator.

Engine Instrument Systems.q: How can a turbine engine exhaust gas temperature system be checked without running the engine?
Engine Instrument Systems.a: By checking the resistance of the thermocouples and circuits.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: What may happen to an oil that is too low in viscosity at engine operating temperatures?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: It is likely to become so thin that the oil film between moving parts is easily broken.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: Why must aircraft reciprocating engines use an oil with a relatively high viscosity?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: Aircraft reciprocating engines use an oil with a relatively high viscosity due to large operating clearances, high operating temperatures, and high bearing pressures.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: What is the primary purpose of an aircraft engine lubricant?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: The primary purpose of a lubricant is to reduce friction between moving parts.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: What are two important characteristics relating to viscosity that an engine oil must possess?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: It must be light enough to circulate freely and heavy enough to provide the proper oil film at engine operating temperatures.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: What areas of a turbine engine oil system are oil screens and filters located?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: Pressure system (main filter), scavenge system, and just before the oil jets (last chance filter).

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: What controls oil pressure in a gear type oil pressure pump?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: An oil pressure relief valve.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: What is the purpose of an oil cooler bypass valve?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: It directs the oil through or around the cooler to keep oil temperature within operating limits.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: What does the presence of metal particles in an engine oil filter indicate?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: Engine internal wear, damage, or possible engine failure.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: What does the oil temperature bulb indicate?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: It indicates the engine oil temperature at the inlet.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: What would be an indication of an obstruction in oil cooler passages?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: Higher than normal oil temperature.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: How much expansion space is required in an oil tank for a reciprocating engine?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: Not less than 10% of tank capacity or 0.5 gallons, whichever is greater.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: What is the most critical lubrication point in a gas turbine engine?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: The exhaust turbine bearing.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: What are two types of turbine engine oil coolers?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: Air-cooled and fuel-cooled.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: What is the purpose of the small sump included on the engine of a dry sump oil system?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: To hold a small supply of oil.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: Where is the temperature bulb located in a dry sump lubricating system?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: Anywhere in the oil inlet line between the supply tank and the engine.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: Where is the oil stored on a dry sump engine?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: In an external tank mounted on the engine or aircraft structure.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: On a dry sump system, where are the chip detectors normally located?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: In the scavenge lines, oil tank, and oil sump.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: Name two contaminants that can be found in oil samples.
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: Gasoline, moisture, acids, dirt, carbon, metallic particles.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: What would be the indications of an inadequate oil supply?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: Low oil pressure with high oil temperature.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: What causes oil foaming?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: Diluted oil, contaminated oil, or oil level too high.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: Why is the exhaust turbine bearing the most critical lubricating point in a gas turbine engine?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: Because of the high temperature normally present.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: Under what conditions will oil flow through the bypass jacket instead of the core of an oil cooler?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: When the oil is cold or the core is blocked with thick, congealed oil.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: What is the purpose of the oil cooler flow control valve?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: It determines the path the oil takes through the cooler.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: What does the presence of metal particles in an engine pressure or scavenge filter screen indicate?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: Possible internal wear, damage, or engine failure.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: What procedures should be followed when disassembling and reassembling an oil screen assembly?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: The manufacturer’s procedures.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: After performing maintenance on an oil system, how long should the engine be run before checking the oil level?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: At least two minutes.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: If an oil pressure gauge is suspected of being inaccurate, what should be done?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: Replace the gauge.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: An engine is showing low oil pressure with no leaks. What may cause this?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: Insufficient oil, dirty strainers, defective gauge, air lock, dirt in relief valve, leaks in suction/pressure lines, high oil temp, stoppage in pump intake, worn/scored bearings.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: When high oil temperature is reported and oil quantity is normal, what are the possible causes?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: Insufficient air cooling, insufficient oil supply, clogged lines/strainers, failing bearings, defective thermostat, defective gauge, excessive blow-by.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: In a reciprocating engine, what is the result if the oil cooler bypass valve remains stuck in the full open position?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: Oil will continue to bypass the core of the cooler.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: What factors must an engine manufacturer consider when determining proper oil grade?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: Operating load, rotational speeds, and operating temperatures.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: What are some oil contaminants?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: Moisture, acids, dirt, carbon, metal particles.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: What type of oil is used in turbine engine oil systems?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: Synthetic oil.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: What could cause excessive oil consumption without visible leaks in a reciprocating engine?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: Worn or broken piston rings.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: Why is an oil tank required to have expansion space?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: To allow for thermal expansion and air in the return oil.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: What is the function of a dispersant additive in ashless dispersant oil?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: Keeps sludge-forming contaminants in suspension until filtered or drained.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: What is the first thing to do if metal particles are found in an engine oil filter?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: Evaluate the finding.

Engine Lubrication Systems.q: In accordance with manufacturer’s instructions, what can cause oil foaming?
Engine Lubrication Systems.a: Diluted oil, contaminated oil, or oil level too high.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: What is the purpose of a magneto?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: The purpose of a magneto is to produce and deliver a high-tension spark across the electrodes of each spark plug in each cylinder.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: Where is the E-gap position in a magneto?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: The E-gap, or efficiency gap, position, or angle is several degrees of magnet rotation beyond the magnet's neutral position.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: What is the position of the engine crankshaft when ignition occurs?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: The ignition event occurs when the piston is a specified number of crankshaft degrees before top dead center on the compression stroke.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: What are the three main components of a spark plug?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: The electrode, the insulator, and the outer shell.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: What is the term "heat range" referred to regarding a spark plug?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: The heat range of a spark plug is a measure of the plug's ability to transfer the heat of combustion to the cylinder head.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: What is the function of the spark plug in the ignition system?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: To conduct a short impulse of high voltage current inside the combustion chamber to ignite the fuel charge.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: In many ignition systems, what component is used to furnish a delayed spark for starting a reciprocating engine?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: An impulse coupling.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: What are the three major circuits of a high-tension magneto system?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: The magnetic circuit, the primary circuit, and the secondary circuit.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: What type of ignition system found on some newer reciprocating engines requires no magnetos or auxiliary starting system?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: A solid-state ignition system (electronic ignition).

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: What is the function of the FADEC system?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: It continuously monitors and controls ignition, timing, fuel mixture, and spark ignition.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: During the start mode of a starter generator, what are the three field windings used?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: The C field, compensating, and commutating windings.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: Name two parts of a starter generator that commonly require periodic inspection.
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: The commutator and brushes.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: As a general rule, when should starter brushes be replaced?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: When worn down to approximately one-half the original length.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: Name three sources of high volume, low pressure, compressed air commonly used for starting turbine aircraft with air turbine starters.
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: Ground-operated air cart, the APU, or a cross-bleed start from an engine already operating.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: What are the components of a high-tension magnetic circuit?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: A permanent multipole rotating magnet, a soft iron core, and pole shoes.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: What are the three events required to fire a spark plug in a cylinder?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: Magneto in the E-gap position, breaker points opening, and distributor aligned correctly.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: What is the first concern when replacing or preparing a magneto for installation?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: The internal timing of the magneto.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: When the ignition switch is off in a magneto system, what is the condition of the ground circuit?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: The ground circuit is completed through the switch to ground.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: What happens when a magnetic rotor is in the E-gap position and the breaker points open?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: The spark plugs fire.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: Why are turbine engine igniters less susceptible to carbon fouling?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: Because of the higher temperature of the high-intensity spark.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: What are typically the three main components of turbine engine ignition systems?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: Exciters, ignition leads, and igniters.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: What four conditions must be in sync for ignition timing?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: 1) Piston in #1 cylinder positioned a set number of degrees before TDC on compression stroke, 2) rotating magnet in E-gap position, 3) breaker points just opening on #1 cam lobe, 4) distributor finger aligned with #1 cylinder electrode.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: What are the two classifications of aircraft magneto-ignition systems?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: High-tension or low-tension.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: How does the low tension system virtually eliminate flashover?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: By eliminating air gaps in the distributor with a brush type distributor; high voltage is present only in short leads between the transformer and spark plug.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: What is the most common cause of spark plug fouling?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: Excessively rich idle mixtures.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: How many spark plugs does a 6-cylinder engine have?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: 12 spark plugs.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: What does the high-tension magneto primary electrical circuit consist of?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: Breaker contact points, a condenser, and an insulated coil.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: What does the term E-gap describe?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: The number of degrees beyond the neutral position a rotor magnet pole should be to obtain the strongest spark at breaker point separation.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: When using a magneto timing light to check a magneto installed on the aircraft, what position should the ignition switch be in?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: The "on" position.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: What dual purpose do ignition harness leads serve?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: They provide a conductor path for high-tension voltage to the spark plug and also shield against stray magnetic fields.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: How can an igniter’s operation be checked without removing it?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: By listening for a snapping noise as the engine begins to turn with the starter.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: In starter generators, which windings ordinarily receive current in the start mode?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: Both the field and the armature.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: What does a magneto timing light indicate?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: The exact instant that the magneto points open.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: What is the purpose of the capacitor in a magneto?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: It prevents arcing at the points and aids in collapsing the magnetic field.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: What is the purpose of using two magnetos in an engine’s ignition system?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: System redundancy for safety and improved combustion efficiency.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: Which magneto is grounded when the magneto switch is selected to the right magneto?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: The left magneto.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: How can the remaining service life of starter generator brushes be determined?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: By visual inspection of the amount of wear groove remaining.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: What is the function of a duty cycle in turbine engine ignition systems?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: It allows operation of the ignition system for a given time followed by a specified cooling period.

Ignition and Starting Systems.q: Why is it not common practice to check an igniter by removing it and noting the spark during a start cycle?
Ignition and Starting Systems.a: Because the high energy and voltage of turbine ignition systems can cause injury or death to personnel.

Propellers.q: On light aircraft propellers, what is the effect of centrifugal force acting on propeller blade counterweights?
Propellers.a: Centrifugal force acting on blade counterweights increases blade pitch.

Propellers.q: How is feathering accomplished on a constant speed propeller?
Propellers.a: Feathering is accomplished by releasing the governor oil pressure from the propeller.

Propellers.q: How is the pitch of a fixed pitch propeller adjusted?
Propellers.a: It may only be adjusted by an appropriately rated propeller repair station or the manufacturer.

Propellers.q: What happens to a constant speed feathering propeller when the propeller governor oil pressure drops to zero?
Propellers.a: The propeller will feather.

Propellers.q: What is the purpose of an oil accumulator connected to a propeller governor?
Propellers.a: To assist in providing oil pressure to the propeller to unfeather the blades.

Propellers.q: Other than maintaining a constant speed, what are two other functions that can be found on a controllable variable pitch propeller?
Propellers.a: It can also be put into the positions of feather and reverse.

Propellers.q: With a constant speed propeller, what blade angle will occur that is pitch controlled by a propeller governor during a constant power dive?
Propellers.a: The blade angle will increase to prevent overspeeding.

Propellers.q: What are the terms alpha range and beta range refer to?
Propellers.a: Both terms pertain to the operating modes of a turboprop reversible pitch propeller.

Propellers.q: What is the beta range of a turboprop propeller?
Propellers.a: The ground range below flight idle to full reverse.

Propellers.q: What positions of a two-bladed propeller are used during a static balance test?
Propellers.a: First with the blades in a vertical position and then with the blades in a horizontal position.

Propellers.q: How should aluminum propeller blades and hubs be cleaned?
Propellers.a: In accordance with the manufacturer’s recommendations.

Propellers.q: When engine vibration is reported, what should be checked to determine if it is caused by the engine or propeller?
Propellers.a: Check the blade tracking and blade angle setting.

Propellers.q: What is the primary purpose of the front and rear cones for propellers that are installed on spline shafts?
Propellers.a: To position the propeller hub on the spline shaft.

Propellers.q: When a propeller is damaged beyond repair and needs to be replaced, where is the approved same make and model or alternate propeller specified?
Propellers.a: In the type certificate data sheet (TCDS).

Propellers.q: What system allows a pilot to reduce noise and vibration by adjusting the phase angle between the propellers on the aircraft’s engines?
Propellers.a: The propeller synchrophasing system.

Propellers.q: What two types of systems are used for propeller ice control?
Propellers.a: Fluid (for anti-icing systems) and electric (for de-icing systems).

Propellers.q: Stresses on the propeller increase in proportion to what?
Propellers.a: They increase in proportion to the RPM.

Propellers.q: Define the term propeller slip.
Propellers.a: The difference between the geometric pitch of the propeller and its effective pitch.

Propellers.q: What may be the indication of an out-of-track propeller?
Propellers.a: Vibrations that can be felt in the airframe.

Propellers.q: When should a propeller be overhauled?
Propellers.a: At the maximum hours or calendar time limit, whichever occurs first (established by manufacturer).

Propellers.q: Can a powerplant mechanic perform a major repair to a propeller?
Propellers.a: No, certificated powerplant mechanics are not allowed to perform major repairs on propellers on a standard category aircraft.

Propellers.q: When should engine tachometer accuracy be verified on engines with a metal propeller?
Propellers.a: At 100-hour intervals or at annual inspection, whichever occurs first.

Propellers.q: On an aluminum propeller, what type of crack is immediate cause for rejection?
Propellers.a: A transverse crack or flaw of any size.

Propellers.q: What may cause a propeller to be out-of-track?
Propellers.a: Bent blades, a bent propeller flange, or propeller mounting bolts that are over- or under-torqued.

Propellers.q: Why should you not use metal scribes or other sharply pointed instruments to mark propeller blades?
Propellers.a: Because such surface scratches can eventually result in blade failure.

Propellers.q: In determining blade angle using a universal propeller protractor, 10 points on the vernier scale are equal to how many points on the degree scale?
Propellers.a: 9 points.

Propellers.q: What is the purpose of propellers?
Propellers.a: To produce thrust to push or pull the aircraft through the air.

Propellers.q: What is a fixed-pitch propeller?
Propellers.a: A propeller with a built-in blade angle that cannot be changed.

Propellers.q: What is blade tracking?
Propellers.a: Determining the positions of the propeller blade tips in relation to each other.

Propellers.q: What tool may be used to determine blade angle?
Propellers.a: A universal propeller protractor.

Propellers.q: What two forces are in balance in a propeller governor in an on-speed condition?
Propellers.a: The speeder spring and the flyweights.

Propellers.q: What type of non-destructive testing should be accomplished to a propeller after blending?
Propellers.a: The non-destructive testing procedure recommended by the manufacturer.

Propellers.q: What can be used to clean a wooden propeller?
Propellers.a: Warm water and a mild soap with a brush and cloth.

Propellers.q: What may be used to remove repair file marks from aluminum propellers?
Propellers.a: Very fine sandpaper.

Propellers.q: What are the criteria used by manufacturers to determine the amount of bend damage that can be repaired by cold bending of aluminum propellers?
Propellers.a: The extent of the bend and the blade station location.

Propellers.q: What is the alpha range of a turboprop?
Propellers.a: The operation in the constant speed mode, usually in flight.

Propellers.q: On light aircraft propellers, what is the effect of centrifugal force acting on blade counterweights?
Propellers.a: It serves to increase blade pitch.

Propellers.q: What does a typical constant speed propeller governor accomplish when operating?
Propellers.a: It boosts engine oil pressure before it enters the propeller hub, controls oil flow to the propeller, and senses engine rotational speed.

Reciprocating Engines.q: Before starting a reciprocating engine with a manifold pressure gauge, what should be observed on the gauge prior to starting the engine?
Reciprocating Engines.a: The manifold pressure gauge should read approximate atmospheric barometric pressure when the engine is not running.

Reciprocating Engines.q: What should be done to repair a loose stud in an engine crankcase?
Reciprocating Engines.a: Remove the loose stud, inspect the hole for size and condition of the threads. It may be repaired using a helicoil insert or if acceptable a suitable oversized stud.

Reciprocating Engines.q: What does the diesel cycle engine depend on to provide for the ignition of the air fuel charge in the cylinder?
Reciprocating Engines.a: It depends on high compression pressures.

Reciprocating Engines.q: How is a cylinder barrel inspected for out-of-roundness?
Reciprocating Engines.a: A dial indicator, a telescopic gauge and micrometer, or an inside micrometer can be used to measure at the top of the cylinder and also at the skirt. Two readings should be taken, 90 degrees to each other.

Reciprocating Engines.q: What are reciprocating engine subdivisions based on?
Reciprocating Engines.a: They are based on the type of cylinder arrangement.

Reciprocating Engines.q: What is valve overlap?
Reciprocating Engines.a: It is when the intake and exhaust valves are open at the same time.

Reciprocating Engines.q: Where are cracks most likely to be found in a piston?
Reciprocating Engines.a: Highly stressed areas, the base of the pin bosses, inside at the junction of the head and walls, and the ring land.

Reciprocating Engines.q: Name the four strokes of a reciprocating engine.
Reciprocating Engines.a: Intake stroke, compression stroke, power stroke, exhaust stroke.

Reciprocating Engines.q: What is meant when it is said that an engine part is within serviceable limits?
Reciprocating Engines.a: A part that is within serviceable limits will not likely wear to the point of causing engine failure within the next time between overhaul interval.

Reciprocating Engines.q: What purpose does valve overlap serve in a reciprocating engine?
Reciprocating Engines.a: Valve overlap provides better volumetric efficiency and lowers the cylinder operating temperature.

Reciprocating Engines.q: How many revolutions of the crankshaft are required for every four strokes?
Reciprocating Engines.a: The crankshaft rotates two complete revolutions for every four strokes.

Reciprocating Engines.q: What inspections should be performed after a propeller strike without sudden stoppage?
Reciprocating Engines.a: Inspect engine mounts, the crankcase and nose section for damage. Inspect the oil and oil filters and screens for metal particles. Inspect the crankshaft or drive shaft for cracks or misalignment.

Reciprocating Engines.q: What is the purpose of oil control piston rings in a reciprocating engine?
Reciprocating Engines.a: Oil control piston rings regulate the thickness of the oil film on the cylinder wall.

Reciprocating Engines.q: When an engine is overhauled, how is its total time affected?
Reciprocating Engines.a: The total time on the engine must be continued and the time since major overhaul is entered into the engine log.

Reciprocating Engines.q: What types of bearings are generally found in reciprocating engines?
Reciprocating Engines.a: Plain, roller, and ball bearings.

Reciprocating Engines.q: On engines equipped with hydraulic valve lifters, what should the running valve clearance be?
Reciprocating Engines.a: Zero.

Reciprocating Engines.q: Name some of the precautions that should be performed prior to engine removal.
Reciprocating Engines.a: Disconnect the battery, turn off the fuel valves, chock the wheels, and if necessary, install a tail stand.

Reciprocating Engines.q: What are the different types of piston rings used in reciprocating engines?
Reciprocating Engines.a: Compression rings, oil control rings, oil scraper rings.

Reciprocating Engines.q: If a weak cylinder is found during a compression check, what must be inspected?
Reciprocating Engines.a: An internal inspection of the cylinder must be completed to determine the cause.

Reciprocating Engines.q: What is the purpose of valve overlap in a high-performance air-cooled reciprocating engine?
Reciprocating Engines.a: Valve overlap aids in volumetric efficiency.

Reciprocating Engines.q: What is the purpose of crankshaft dynamic dampers?
Reciprocating Engines.a: Dynamic dampers reduce vibration during engine operation.

Reciprocating Engines.q: What could be the causes of low compression in a cylinder?
Reciprocating Engines.a: Valves not seating properly, piston rings not seating correctly, piston or cylinder damage.

Reciprocating Engines.q: On a reciprocating aircraft engine, what are two indications of induction system leakage?
Reciprocating Engines.a: Inability to idle properly and reduced power output.

Reciprocating Engines.q: What is an indication that a primer is leaking or is open during engine operation?
Reciprocating Engines.a: The engine will not idle properly.

Reciprocating Engines.q: When troubleshooting a running engine malfunction, what does a cold cylinder indicate?
Reciprocating Engines.a: Lack of combustion or incomplete combustion within the cylinder.

Reciprocating Engines.q: When is the advisory circular 43.13-1 considered acceptable for the inspection and repair of non-pressurized civil aircraft?
Reciprocating Engines.a: Only when there are no manufacturer repair or maintenance instructions.

Reciprocating Engines.q: What procedure should be done if a hissing sound is heard when pulling the propeller through on a reciprocating engine?
Reciprocating Engines.a: Perform a cylinder compression test to identify the faulty cylinder.

Reciprocating Engines.q: Name two types of damage that can be found on a piston during inspection.
Reciprocating Engines.a: Cracked skirts, broken ring land, deep scoring on piston pin holes.

Reciprocating Engines.q: When installing piston rings on a piston, how should the ring gaps be aligned relative to each other?
Reciprocating Engines.a: Ring gaps should be staggered around the piston to prevent excessive blow-by.

Reciprocating Engines.q: When an engine is rebuilt by the manufacturer, how is its operating history affected?
Reciprocating Engines.a: A rebuilt engine is considered to have no previous operating history and can be issued a zero-time record.

Reciprocating Engines.q: What is the purpose of performing a compression test on a reciprocating engine?
Reciprocating Engines.a: To determine if the valves, piston rings, and pistons are adequately sealing the combustion chamber.

Reciprocating Engines.q: What are two conditions that could be the result of worn or incorrectly installed piston rings?
Reciprocating Engines.a: Low cylinder compression and excessive oil consumption.

Reciprocating Engines.q: What is the final step of preparing a reciprocating engine for long-term storage?
Reciprocating Engines.a: Attach a sign stating “Do not turn crankshaft, engine preserved, and with the preservation date.”

Reciprocating Engines.q: Where are straight roller bearings used in reciprocating aircraft engines?
Reciprocating Engines.a: High-power reciprocating aircraft engines for crankshaft main bearings.

Reciprocating Engines.q: What types of loads are straight roller bearings designed to be used for?
Reciprocating Engines.a: Radial loads only.

Reciprocating Engines.q: Chrome-plated cylinders are usually identified by a paint band around the barrel. What color is this band?
Reciprocating Engines.a: International orange.

Reciprocating Engines.q: Why is there a greater amount of fin area on the exhaust portion of the cylinder?
Reciprocating Engines.a: The exhaust valve region is the hottest part of the internal surface, so more fin area is provided there.

Reciprocating Engines.q: What type of rings are used in chromium-plated cylinders?
Reciprocating Engines.a: Cast-iron rings.

Reciprocating Engines.q: When checked for static balance, what is indicated if the crankshaft tends to turn toward any one position?
Reciprocating Engines.a: It is out of static balance.

Reciprocating Engines.q: When is a crankshaft considered dynamically balanced?
Reciprocating Engines.a: When all forces created by rotation and power impulses are balanced within themselves, producing little or no vibration during operation.

Reciprocating Engines.q: What type of connecting rods are commonly found in radial engines?
Reciprocating Engines.a: Master and articulating rod assemblies.

Reciprocating Engines.q: Are the connecting rods used in each cylinder of a radial engine the same? Explain.
Reciprocating Engines.a: No, radial engines use master and articulating rod assemblies.

Reciprocating Engines.q: What is used to help prevent valve surge or valve floating in an aircraft engine?
Reciprocating Engines.a: Two or more springs are used on each valve.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What problem would be caused during engine operation with an excessive amount of cooling fin missing from a cylinder?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: It will cause a hot spot in the cylinder.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What could the effects be if the temperature inside the reciprocating engine cylinder is excessive?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: The fuel-air mixture is preheated and combustion occurs before the desired time.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What should be done to a cylinder with excessive cooling fin area broken off?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: The cylinder should be replaced.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What is used to transfer the cylinder heat from the surface area to the atmosphere for air-cooled reciprocating engines?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: Cooling fins are used.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What information must be consulted prior to reprofiling a cooling fin?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: Consult the manufacturer's service or overhaul manual for the allowable limits.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What may be the indication of leaking intake pipes?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: The engine will run rough, most noticeable at low RPM.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What are the two indications of an induction system obstruction?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: The engine will not start, or it will have reduced power when the throttle is advanced.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What happens to engine heat after it is transferred to the cylinder cooling fins?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: The heat is transferred out of the engine into the air.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What is the common method used to prevent induction system ice in reciprocating engines?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: By raising the temperature of the induction air using a carburetor heat system.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: Where does throttle ice form?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: On the rear side of the throttle plate.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: Why is it important to have the cowl flaps adjusted correctly?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: To keep cylinder head temperatures within allowable limits.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: Why do some aircraft use augmenters?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: To provide additional cooling airflow.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What is the approximate percentage of air passing through a turbine engine that is used for cooling rather than combustion?
How’s compressor bleed air used to effectively allow higher gas temperatures in the turbine section of some engines?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: It is directed through hollow turbine inlet guide vanes and blades to keep them cool.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What are the functions of engine cowling?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: To streamline the engine area for reduced drag and control airflow for cooling.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What is the relationship between turbine engine upper temperature limits and power produced?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: The higher the allowable combustion temperature, the more power the engine can produce.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What are the three classifications of induction system ice?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: Impact ice, fuel evaporation ice, and throttle ice.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: When is carburetor throttle ice most likely to form due to throttle position?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: When the throttle is partially closed.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: Where must most cooling airflow be directed to cool a turbine engine?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: Through the inside of the engine.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What areas of a turbine engine are cooled by air passing through the engine?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: The combustion chamber and turbine.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What is the source of bleed air vented to turbine engine bearings and other parts in some engines?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: The engine compressor.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What causes fuel evaporation ice?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: The drop in temperature due to fuel evaporation after being introduced into the airstream.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: At what point does an internally driven supercharger boost air pressure?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: After the carburetor, by compressing the air-fuel mixture.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What are the two types of supercharged induction systems?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: Internally driven and externally driven (turbo-supercharged).

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What are the two causes of a turbocharged engine surging?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: Malfunction of either the controller or the wastegate.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What are the three main parts of a turbine supercharger?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: Compressor assembly, turbine wheel assembly, and full floating shaft bearing assembly.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What is the result if a turbocharger wastegate will not close fully?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: The aircraft will not be able to reach its critical altitude.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What is used on many turbocharged engines for the wastegate actuator power?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: Engine oil.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What is the function of an augmenter cooling system?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: It uses exhaust gas velocity to cause airflow over the engine.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What is an indication of a dirty air inlet filter?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: Loss of power or hard starting.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What is the danger of applying carburetor heat with high engine power?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: Detonation may occur.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What engine power indication will occur when carburetor heat is applied to an operating engine?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: A drop in RPM and loss of power.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What is used to control the amount of airflow through the cooling area of a reciprocating engine?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: Cowl flaps.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What is the purpose of cowl air seals?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: To direct air through the baffle system.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What is used in the absence of fins in a liquid-cooled engine?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: Water jackets.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: Blast tubes are built into the baffles to do what?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: Direct cooling air onto the rear spark plug elbows to prevent overheating.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: If the cylinder cooling fin damage exceeds the manufacturer's limits, what corrective action must be taken?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: The cylinder must be replaced.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What are the possible problems that are noted due to air intake leaks on a reciprocating engine?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: Engine fails to start, runs rough, produces low power, or idles improperly.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: Define manifold pressure and what is it measured in?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: It is the average absolute pressure of the intake charge, measured in inches of mercury.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What throttle positions are most favorable to ice forming in the carburetor throttle area?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: Closed throttle and part throttle.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What are the three units in a reciprocating engine induction system?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: Carburetor, air scoop, and intake manifold.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: How does a divergent-shaped jet engine inlet affect the air flowing in subsonic flight?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: It decreases air velocity and increases air pressure.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: The Venturi-type particle separator found on turbine helicopters uses what principle to remove debris?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: Inertia.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: Name two methods used for anti-icing of turbine engine inlet ducts.
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: Engine bleed air and electric heating.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: Bell-mouth inlet ducts are used typically on what type of aircraft?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: Helicopters.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What is the function of a turbocharger wastegate?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: It controls the volume of exhaust gas directed to the turbine.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What is the difference between bootstrapping and overboost?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: Bootstrapping is unregulated power drift in manifold pressure; overboost is exceeding allowable manifold pressure limits.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What is the main function of the aftercooler?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: To cool compressed air.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: From where does an externally driven supercharger derive its power?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: From exhaust gases driving a turbine.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What is the function of a sonic venturi in a turbocharger system?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: To limit turbocharger airflow used for cabin pressurization.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: Where should cowl flaps be positioned during ground operations?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: Fully open.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What is the effect on engine performance when induction system icing occurs?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: Power loss and erratic operation.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What is the purpose of the engine baffles?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: To direct air close to cylinders and prevent stagnant hot spots.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What would be the probable cause of low power with respect to the air filter?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: A dirty air filter that needs cleaning or replacement.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What are the three main reasons that excessive heat is undesirable in any reciprocating engine?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: It affects combustion, weakens parts, and impairs lubrication.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What is the purpose of the carburetor air filter?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: To stop dust and debris from entering the engine.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What is the principal function of engine cowling and baffles?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: To direct airflow over cylinders for cooling.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.q: What type of power is used to operate cowl flaps?
Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.a: Manual, electric, or hydraulic.

Turbine Engine Air Systems.q: With regards to Bernoulli's principle, how does a divergent-shaped jet engine inlet affect the air flowing to the engine when an airplane is in subsonic flight?
Turbine Engine Air Systems.a: It will cause the air velocity to decrease and the air pressure to increase.

Turbine Engine Air Systems.q: What areas of a turbine engine are cooled by air passing through the engine?
Turbine Engine Air Systems.a: Cooling air that passes through the engine is used to cool the combustion chamber in the turbine or hot section.

Turbine Engine Air Systems.q: What device is installed on some aircraft turbine engines to prevent debris damage by utilizing a stream of engine bleed air blown toward the ground ahead of the engine?
What is the source of bleed air vented to turbine engine bearings and other parts of the turbine engine?
Turbine Engine Air Systems.a: Bleed air from the turbine engine compressor section.

Turbine Engine Air Systems.q: Anti-icing of jet engine air inlets is commonly accomplished by what method?
Turbine Engine Air Systems.a: It is accomplished by engine bleed air, ducted through the critical areas, or the use of electric heating elements.

Turbine Engine Air Systems.q: Cooling air inlets are frequently provided around the exterior of a turbine engine to permit the entrance of air. To cool what?
Turbine Engine Air Systems.a: They cool the turbine case, the bearings, and the turbine nozzle.

Turbine Engine Air Systems.q: What is the approximate percentage of air passing through a turbine engine that is used for cooling rather than combustion?
What is the relationship between turbine engine upper temperature limits and power produced?
Turbine Engine Air Systems.a: The higher the combustion temperatures that an engine can withstand without damage, the more power it is capable of producing.

Turbine Engine Air Systems.q: How is compressor bleed air used?
Turbine Engine Air Systems.a: To effectively allow higher gas temperatures in the turbine section of some engines. Bleed air from the compressor is ducted through hollow turbine section inlet guide vanes and hollow first stage rotor blades, keeping them cool enough to prevent damage.

Turbine Engine Air Systems.q: The Venturi-type particle separator, found on many turbine-powered helicopters, utilizes what characteristic of matter to help remove debris from induction air?
Name two methods used for anti-icing of turbine engine inlet ducts.
Turbine Engine Air Systems.a: Engine bleed air and electric heating elements.

Turbine Engine Air Systems.q: What is meant by a convergent inlet duct for a turbine engine?
Turbine Engine Air Systems.a: A convergent duct is one whose cross-sectional area becomes smaller in the direction of the airflow.

Turbine Engine Air Systems.q: What is meant by a divergent inlet duct for a turbine engine?
Turbine Engine Air Systems.a: A divergent duct is one whose cross-sectional area becomes greater in the direction of airflow.

Turbine Engine Air Systems.q: What kind of inlet duct is often used on turbine-powered helicopters?
Why do some turbine engines use variable inlet guide vanes?
Turbine Engine Air Systems.a: Variable inlet guide vanes are automatically adjusted to direct the air into the engine in such a way that it keeps the RPM versus velocity proper for the most efficient operation.

Turbine Engine Air Systems.q: How do some turbine-powered engines prevent ice formation on the inlet guide vanes?
Turbine Engine Air Systems.a: Hot compressor bleed air flows through the hollow inlet guide vanes.

Turbine Engine Air Systems.q: Where is the highest temperature located in a turbine engine?
Turbine Engine Air Systems.a: At the inlet to the high-pressure turbine.

Turbine Engine Air Systems.q: How does an engine air inlet vortex dissipator help prevent foreign object damage to the engine?
Turbine Engine Air Systems.a: A high-velocity stream of compressor bleed air is blown out in front of the engine to break up the vortices that form in front of the engine when it is operating at high power on the ground.

Turbine Engine Air Systems.q: How are the turbine inlet guide vanes and the first stage turbine blades in some turbine engines cooled?
Turbine Engine Air Systems.a: High-pressure compressor bleed air flows through the hollow guide vanes and hollow turbine blades.

Turbine Engines.q: What are the functions of axial flow turbine nozzles?
Turbine Engines.a: The turbine nozzles direct the mass airflow for driving the turbine rotor and deflect the gases to a specific angle.

Turbine Engines.q: What is the function of an APU on modern transport category aircraft?
Turbine Engines.a: To supply ground electrical and pneumatic power to aircraft systems when the main engines are not operating and, in some aircraft, as a backup source of in-flight electrical power.

Turbine Engines.q: What is the major advantage of a turbofan engine over a turbojet engine?
Turbine Engines.a: The production of additional thrust without increasing fuel flow.

Turbine Engines.q: What is shaft horsepower?
Turbine Engines.a: It is an indication of the torque developed by a turboprop or turboshaft engine.

Turbine Engines.q: When is the greatest demand placed on an APU?
Turbine Engines.a: When the APU is supplying bleed air.

Turbine Engines.q: How is an APU generally started?
Turbine Engines.a: With its own electrical starter and battery power.

Turbine Engines.q: What are the desired effects of turbine engine compressor field cleaning?
Turbine Engines.a: Removal of contaminant deposits from interior engine surfaces and improved engine performance.

Turbine Engines.q: What prevents burning of combustion chambers?
Turbine Engines.a: Secondary airflow in the combustion chambers provides a cooling airflow to prevent burning.

Turbine Engines.q: What methods are commonly used to accomplish turbine engine compressor field cleaning?
Turbine Engines.a: Fluid wash and abrasive grit blast.

Turbine Engines.q: What are factors that determine the amount of air passing through a turbine engine?
Turbine Engines.a: Compressor speed, forward speed of the aircraft, and density of the ambient surrounding air.

Turbine Engines.q: Which type of turbine engine design was developed to turn a large fan at the front of the engine, producing about 80% of thrust?
Turbine Engines.a: A turbofan engine.

Turbine Engines.q: When a turbofan or turbojet engine is shut down, what should a mechanic listen for?
Turbine Engines.a: During coast down, any rubbing sound or unusual noises from the engine.

Turbine Engines.q: Where does the APU get its fuel supply?
Turbine Engines.a: From one of the aircraft’s main fuel tanks.

Turbine Engines.q: Name the blade root designs used to attach turbine blades to rotor discs.
Turbine Engines.a: Fir tree root, dovetail root, and bulb type root.

Turbine Engines.q: What type of operating practice could cause thermal shock and possible damage to an APU?
Turbine Engines.a: Abrupt shutdown of the APU when heavily loaded without observing cool-down procedures.

Turbine Engines.q: What are carbon seals used for in turbine engines?
Turbine Engines.a: They are used as oil seals for the rotor shaft bearings.

Turbine Engines.q: Name the uses for APU-produced pneumatic power.
Turbine Engines.a: Engine starting, ground air conditioning, and anti-icing.

Turbine Engines.q: What is a split compressor system?
Turbine Engines.a: A dual/twin spool system connected to the turbine section with two shafts, one inside the other.

Turbine Engines.q: How is an APU’s operating speed maintained despite load variations?
Turbine Engines.a: By the APU’s fuel control automatically adjusting the fuel flow.

Turbine Engines.q: What is the effect of following a required cool-down period before APU shutdown?
Turbine Engines.a: Exhaust gas temperature is lowered and stabilized.

Turbine Engines.q: At what speed does a gas turbine APU operate and how is it maintained?
Turbine Engines.a: At or near rated power regardless of load, with fuel control automatically adjusting fuel flow.
